```

"Clark Morris" <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message 
news:voc9521q2hi343d2d63g38ofka2mj76gvn@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 30 Apr 2006 15:02:49 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sabbath.

Not a crime under Roman law. My point stands. As for the disciples, that is 
not the issue here. We cannot reasonably hold Jesus responsible for the acts 
of the disciples. He wasn't running a private army or war machine.

>>
>>He threw money-changers out of the temple, but there was no law governing
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> control the outer court.

Maybe, but, as far as we know, no Roman police or soldiers arrived to break 
up the scuffle... He was therefore not obviously in violation of Roman Law.
>>
>>When asked about this very question of who had predence, God or The 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> didn't give two hoots about.

That was exactly my point. Jesus did not break the Law of the Land. (I have 
no intention of debating religious law...)


 The "render unto Caesar" referred to
> paying taxes to the Romans.

I know. But it was a trap question, posed by a a member of the Jewish 
establishment, in the hopes of trapping him into supporting something 
illegal, or getting hm to incite people not to pay taxes. He didn't fall for 
it. :-)

In fact, that is a very interesting point in the context of this discussion. 
Howard is saying we should disobey or protest 'bad' laws...and illegal acts 
are OK in doing so.  If the Jews had all refused to pay taxes (as they 
probably would do today) that would have been a protest against the Roman 
occupation. Jesus wisely did not support that idea. Was it because he didn't 
believe the end justifies the means, or was it simply a case of "Don't mess 
with the Romans; they are thugs...don't give them any excuse to start 
killing people..."?

Whatever his reasoning, the course he took was correct morally (the end does 
not justify the means) and physically (no horrible Roman retribution on 
innocent people).

Whether he was God or not, he was certainly a very wise man.

Pete.
```

```
In the discussion below I am saying that both Jewish and Roman law
applied to Jesus.  Jewish law was the equivalent of state or
provincial law while Roman law was the equivalent of national law.  It
was probably further confused by Jewish law probably only applying to
Jews and I would welcome further enlightenment on the nuances and the
enforcement capabilities of Herod and the temple establishment (2
separate entities and possibly sets of law).

<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
```

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcREMOVEewing@CAPSacm.org>
- **Date:** 2006-05-02T05:39:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bgC5g.4657$DT5.3456@newsread3.news.pas.earthlink.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4bm63kF11e0vaU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<sb3f421mq8mhpi8iha3pt36kk1joe5hpki@4ax.com> <4as40rFuhmblU1@individual.net> <8a0i42dsf2ka2hjld11nf0nrf875644jn2@4ax.com> <4ate4fFuquvcU1@individual.net> <qpmp42dkqijrelceh3uv6qg661bcn9qq78@4ax.com> <4b5c4jFvkqf6U1@individual.net> <kdcs42l9vv9kh3v3q0ec5arun6t6c6la5m@4ax.com> <4bejajF119no9U1@individual.net> <ek745253hafm1nblbvb7o2e8quv7bui0l8@4ax.com> <4bes44F11e7mrU1@individual.net> <f4g452paqc4v6pf9vvp4efg5cqli5s2hgf@4ax.com> <4binmrF11m7p2U1@individual.net> <voc9521q2hi343d2d63g38ofka2mj76gvn@4ax.com> <4bm63kF11e0vaU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
...
> 
> In fact, that is a very interesting point in the context of this discussion. 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

I think a more precise position regarding adherence to the law is that 
disobeying even what one believes to be a "bad law" is not an action to 
be taken lightly.  There are always at least two possibilities: (1)you 
might be right; (2)you might be a deluded nut case.  In either event you 
must be sufficiently convinced of the rightness of your cause and of the 
inappropriateness of the law that you are prepared to pay the price of 
disobedience. Not you, but history and others will be the judge of 
whether your "illegal" acts were "OK".  Even if history eventually 
judges you right, you and those you care about may pay a very heavy 
price for your action.

Today we honor the memory of those who gave their lives to call 
attention to the injustice of Jim Crow laws in the U.S., which began the 
process of change toward racial justice; but at the time there were many 
in the South who vilified and attacked those who violated those laws. 
There was also great division in the American Colonies over whether 
disobeying certain English laws was justified.  Today much of that 
disobedience would be considered justified (by those of us in the former 
Colonies at least); although a critical examination of history of that 
period can also easily find incidents of excess on both sides that we 
would not condone today.

To look at the other side, the nut cases: While it may be too early to 
be certain of the final verdict, it is highly likely that history will 
judge with contempt those current leaders who claim to hold the moral 
high ground while advocating the murder of others whose chief "fault" is 
the holding of a different religious faith (e.g., Osama bin Laden and 
Pat Robertson).

As for the reasoning of J.C., I doubt if either of your thoughts were 
his main reason for treating the Roman government with circumspection. 
Jesus made clear on several occasions that he believed himself to be the 
fulfillment of the Old Testament prophesies about the Messiah.  That 
required that he eventually be rejected by Israel, not become a martyr 
because of violation of Roman law.
```

```
> In either event you must be sufficiently convinced of the rightness of 
> your cause and of the inappropriateness of the law that you are prepared 
> to pay the price of disobedience.

This is where my view differs a bit.

I have already established through other posts here that I believe in 
rehabilitation rather than punishment. I don't see penalties (punishment) 
really serving any useful purpose. (They certainly don't deter criminals; it 
just makes them more careful not to get caught...) They make Society feel 
better (but then you have to ask yourself what kind of society feels better 
when one of its members is flogged or incarcerated?  A whole 'nother ball 
game...)

What would be much more productive would be to change a criminal's mind 
about breaking the law. (And I believe the long term aim of the justice 
system should be to do that, so that people can live in, and be productive 
members of, a civilised society. Such a society would value and respect all 
its members. Crime would be seen as anti-social and there would be less of 
it.

It has been very interesting to me to watch the drink-driving campaign on 
television here over the past few years and the effect it has had. The 
attitiude that it was macho and manly to have a few beers then get behind 
the wheel, has turned significantly.  Pretty crisp slogans like "If you 
drink and drive, you're a bloody idiot.", "Friends don't let friends drive 
drunk", and "They drink, we die..."  have been very effective. Road deaths 
have dropped significantly, despite the increase of traffic.

To be fair, it has been backed by increased penalties (including jail, and 
instant disqualification from driving), so maybe punishment does play a 
part...at least until the public psyche gets the idea that this is not 
acceptable behaviour.

Who knows what such a campaign against burlary or assault might achieve? (It 
is now starting on family and child abuse...)

Now, if we don't respect the law and decide it is OK to selectively break 
it, when we are not really criminals, how is it possible to instill the idea 
that breaking the law is counter-productive, in people who ARE really 
criminals?

It isn't. That is why I have stated my position as being absolute on that. 
If you break the law you should realise you are undermining the democratic 
system that made the law in the first place. If that is OK by you, then you 
don't need laws at all and probably shouldn't live in the society of other 
people.

Whether we like it or not, the law is the law. It has to be respected and 
for the most part, obeyed. If you vehemently disagree with a particular law, 
there is a democratic (and legal) process for getting it changed. (persuade 
51% of the population to your pont of view :-)) If you allow a mass 
demonstration, civil unrest, or any other process than the one for legally 
changing the law to succeed in changing the law, there is no point in making 
laws. You might as well accept that mob violence and the power of the strong 
will decide how society is governed. Go back to a Monarchy or Dictatorship 
with absolute rule, or have a military junta who can easily enforce their 
will on the population and therefore don't need a legal process.

>Not you, but history and others will be the judge of whether your "illegal" 
>acts were "OK".  Even if history eventually judges you right, you and those 
>you care about may pay a very heavy price for your action.
>
Exactly. In a free society, we don't want our citizens being martyred. 
That's why we have law.

> Today we honor the memory of those who gave their lives to call attention 
> to the injustice of Jim Crow laws in the U.S., which began the process of 
> change toward racial justice; but at the time there were many in the South 
> who vilified and attacked those who violated those laws.

I have always predicated my argument on free speech and liberty. If these 
are not available, then it probably is time to break out the weapons. I 
don't know what legal recourse was available to the Negro people at the 
time, but I suspect there wasn't much. I don't know if anybody tried to get 
these laws changed by peaceful means and what success, if any, they had.

> There was also great division in the American Colonies over whether 
> disobeying certain English laws was justified.  Today much of that 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> would not condone today.

George was a badly advised and possibly insane monarch, but he was still 
responsible to Parliament. Had the Colonists had proper representation in 
Parliament,as they should have had, the whole unpleasantness could probably 
have been avoided.

As an outside observer with no axe to grind,  I believe the Revolution was 
justified.

(So did Sir Francis Dashwood, the current Baronet at the time, who hosted 
Ben Frankin at the family seat,West Wycombe manor (Franklin's portrait hangs 
there to this day), and gave him money to back the Revolution.  This was 
treason and he would have been summarily executed if it had become known. He 
was also the leader of a cult (The Hellfire Club) to which the King 
belonged. They were troubled times...)

>
> To look at the other side, the nut cases: While it may be too early to be 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robertson).

I think where Bin Lden is concerned it goes a bit beyond holding a different 
faith. He felt (and still feels) it is perfectly OK to murder unarmed people 
in the pursuit of a political goal.  OK, you could argue that Bush does too, 
but I don't think he deliberately targets civilians. His intentions are 
good. Peace, stability, and democracy. Unfortunately, he forgot to ask the 
people of Afghanistan and Iraq if they shared these goals before invading 
their countries.... If he gets Bin Laden, his popularity will soar.

It comes back to this crime and punishment thing. I won't cry for Bin Laden 
if he's caught and executed; what he did was SO anti-social he showed he was 
completely unfit to live in a civilised society... not just ours, 
anybody's...  And there is zero chance he will ever change his mind or try 
to undo the damage. In a case like that, there isn't much point in wasting 
taxpayers' money on keeping him alive. But, I also don't see how executing 
him will make any difference to the thousands of Americans who died on 9/11. 
Perhaps it will make their families feel better (and that is sad too).


>
> As for the reasoning of J.C., I doubt if either of your thoughts were his 
> main reason for treating the Roman government with circumspection. Jesus 
> made clear on several occasions that he believed himself to be the 
> fulfillment of the Old Testament prophesies about the Messiah.

I've never been able to find anywhere in the Gospels where he actually said 
that unequivocally.  Maybe you can set me straight here? It sems to have 
been said for him by others.  It's a pretty simple message: "Look, I'm 
actually God and I'm here to fulfill the prophecy of the Messiah, you've 
been waiting for..."  Never happened. He talked about his Father as being 
God, not himself. That could be the allegory of any Holy Man.

Too many questions for me, I'm afraid...


> That required that he eventually be rejected by Israel, not become a 
> martyr because of violation of Roman law.

Well, if he was God he would have known all along what the outcome was to 
be. What point then in asking why he was forsaken when on the cross?

If he was a deluded man, who believed as you describe, then your point is a 
fair one. We will never know.

Last post from me on this as it is not going to achieve anything, other than 
upset people who really care, and I don't...

(Aplogies to anyone offended; offence was not the purpose of this post...)

Pete.
```

```
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> Variations of this philosophy have always been popular among those
>> with inherited wealth and power.   And since they have the power,

powerful.    If you are neither, then my observation wasn't about what
is popular with you.

and powerful.

powerful.    I am neither wealthy nor powerful.    It is not at all
obvious to me how you inferred that I support such a philosophy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any comments? (Answers to Pete)

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-15T07:55:26-06:00

```
> On Thu, 6 Apr 2006 02:28:44 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> But not against men?

OK, against the weak and defenceless... (this isn't quite the same. There is 
an idea I had instilled in me that women and children should be protected 
and nurtured. Old-fashioned, I know, but I adhere to it...)
>
>>or murders innocent people by acts of terrorism:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Brave things" like leaving a parcel in a crowded pub and killing and 
maiming teenagers who are enjoying a quiet Sunday drink?  "Brave things" 
like flying aircraft into buildings, in the certain knowledge of going to 
Heaven? Or, machine gunning school children in the furtherance of a 
political cause?

Sorry Howard, I know who the terrorists are, and I don't need some American 
politician to decide for me.

I lived in London while it was under attack from such people; they call 
themselves 'soldiers' but it is an affront to anyone who has ever been a 
soldier.  Soldiers don't murder innocent civilians (or even enemy who have 
laid down their weapons).

I'd be perfectly happy to see such people put against a wall and shot like 
the 'soldiers' they claim to be.

> If someone murders innocent people by acts of greed, or acts of war,
> or acts of terrorism, the result is the same, so why make the
> distinction when picking punishments?
>

For the same reason that we make a distinction between someone stealing food 
from a supermarket and someone stealing cash from the same supermarket. 
Because the Law is required to consider the whole picture and temper justice 
with mercy.

Except for terrorism... :-)

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<8a3df$441a31ae$45491d7a$26992@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142569355.342927.312930@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <d3560$441c35ac$45491d7a$23317@KNOLOGY.NET> <1142723990.730895.314110@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <dcaa9$441cc5e9$45491d7a$12023@KNOLOGY.NET> <icht12pk1gen5lib7fv0nl16e7i2e3bv1t@4ax.com> <f2d84$441f4c20$45491d7a$24587@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvpkie$snq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5d1e7$44209f40$45491d7a$14101@KNOLOGY.NET> <dvs60k$2edc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tn0h62psgjkilpf4il4mnnn63er513enab@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:tn0h62psgjkilpf4il4mnnn63er513enab@4ax.com...

> But aren't all English language scriptures adjusted?

Yes.  Even the English text of the Book of Mormon has had some grammatical 
rough edges -- rectifying ambiguities in some cases -- cleaned up since its 
original publication in 1829!  ;-)

Seriously, there's a supportable argument that even the "original texts" 
(either Old or New Testament) have not survived without any shred of 
controversy as to their literal, or for that matter theological, content. 
There is no "the" Hebrew/Aramaic text for the Old Testament (though many use 
the Masoretic Text), nor is there a "the" text for the New (though some hold 
the Textus Receptus as the culmination of God's effort to rectify the 
problems in the Greek, and others hold with the most recent edition of the 
UBS/Nestle-Aland text as representing the best scholarship).

But in any event I try to refer back to what most (not all) scholars hold is 
most likely closest to "original text", in the original languages, with side 
glances at other witnesses to their contents (the Septuagint in Greek for 
the Old Testament, and primarily the Vulgate and other early Latin witnesses 
for the New).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 21 Mar 2006 18:50:24 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>My *point*, if you read the original thread, was about whether it's 
>possible to "study" a religion without being proselytized by it.  I 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>(Personally, I think it is.  I have yet to see Mr. Brazee's reply, though.)

Is that above the question you're waiting for a reply from me about?

Certainly it is possible to "study" a religion without being
proselytized by it.    And our students should have familiarity with
the major religions of the world.    

It is very difficult for a person who believes strongly in one
religion teach all of the religions equally well though, so much so
that I wouldn't want to force him to do so.   A variety of teachers
should be used for this purpose.
```

```
On Fri, 28 Apr 2006 21:57:41 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

>Not sure if you've heard of Ann Coulter - yes, she's unashamedly 
>right-wing, but she's also quite scholarly.  Her book "Treason" was a 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>*was* receiving communications from the USSR.

So what legal definition did she use to define the USSR as "the
enemy"?

In the absence of a declared war, anybody could be considered "the
enemy", including people of opposition parties.    Before we can
reinstate treason in practice we need to come up with a new way of
defining "the enemy".

But even before - we haven't punished Red Cross workers who have given
"aid and comfort to" people in states defined as "the enemy".    Heck,
we've had soldiers assisting people in occupied Germany before it
surrendered in WWII.    That wasn't considered treasonous.

I'm not sure a better definition will be sufficient.   Our culture has
changed so that Heresy is no longer persecuted the way it once was.
Both Treason and Heresy are big in Muslim countries, but not in
Christian countries anymore.

Note:    A friend at my work just got back from his 2nd charitable
trip to India in two years, supplying medical supplies and care to the
needy.   This was a Christian enterprise, paid for by his church. I'm
not aware of such outreach programs by other communities (including
Islamic).     A change of emphasis over the centuries from conversion
by the sword to doing good has significant.
```

- **References:** `<20bae$44483566$45491de7$16077@KNOLOGY.NET> <4at6u9Fuu907U1@individual.net> <e2r6qi$1k84$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Wq94g.132937$BL7.65413@fe09.news.easynews.com> <e2rbu8$1nb0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4bekolF118mmaU1@individual.net> <80173$4452d627$45491dc8$10985@KNOLOGY.NET> <tr2c52pa6jcvo5mjr3fj34kie7bn35hdbf@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Fri, 28 Apr 2006 21:57:41 -0500, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> enemy"?

To be as old as you are, and not accept that the USSR was our enemy - I 
just don't know what to say to that.  Perhaps you've forgotten...  ;)

> In the absence of a declared war, anybody could be considered "the
> enemy", including people of opposition parties.    Before we can
> reinstate treason in practice we need to come up with a new way of
> defining "the enemy".

It's actually quite easy.  In the government's eyes, anyone trying to 
overthrow it or defeat it militarily (either force-on-force, or 
guerrilla tactics) is the enemy.

> But even before - we haven't punished Red Cross workers who have given
> "aid and comfort to" people in states defined as "the enemy".    Heck,
> we've had soldiers assisting people in occupied Germany before it
> surrendered in WWII.    That wasn't considered treasonous.

"The enemy" is an entity, not people - and health care is specifically 
addressed in the Geneva Convention and the other generally accepted laws 
of war.  Combatants (both lawful and unlawful) are viewed as 
representatives of one of the aforementioned "enemies", and are fair 
game.  However, if we capture them alive, or they surrender, we *must* 
take them in, feed them, and offer them the same medical care we provide 
for our own troops.

With this distinction, "aid and comfort" to citizens of "the enemy" is 
not treasonous; neither is the American Red Cross caring for a wounded 
soldier of "the enemy".  Quartering "the enemies" troops, though, would 
be, as would spying for "the enemy" or sabotaging our equipment.

Not sure that the WBC pukes qualify under this definition, as they're 
attempting to demoralize the troops; although, psychological 
demoralization during a time of war *could* be stretched to apply - 
although sedition.  Had our country been zealously prosecuting traitors 
over the years, it might work - however, in the current environment, I 
don't think it would fly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Philosphical" question on "succesful" file status testing

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-01T07:44:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o34c52h474gsk2gmb18o1rs0l8t6mtgvqm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<M_M4g.173857$zy2.141605@fe08.news.easynews.com> <e314s9$lb7$1@reader1.panix.com> <nGY4g.221$X16.66@fe07.lga>`

```
On Sun, 30 Apr 2006 01:24:50 -0500, Last Boy Scout <eggbtr@ezl.com>
wrote:

>I think it might be right on.
>When I look at a list of status codes in my IBM Manual the second digit 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>if you are looking at a read it might save some time.


http://www.simotime.com/vsmfsk01.htm

Status
Key-1  	Condition  	Status
Key-2  	Description
			
0 	Successful
Completion 	0 	No further information
  	  	2 	The input-output statement was successfully
executed, but a duplicate key was detected. For a READ statement the
key value for the current key of reference was equal to the value of
the same key in the next record within the current key of reference.
For a REWRITE or WRITE statement, the record just written created a
duplicate key value for at least one alternate record key for which
duplicates are allowed.
  	  	4 	A READ statement was successfully executed,
but the length of the record being processed did not conform to the
fixed file attributes for that file.
  	  	5 	An OPEN statement is successfully executed but
the referenced optional file is not present at the time the OPEN
statement is executed. If the open mode is I-O, the file has been
created. Under MVS, the file has been created if the open mode is I-O
or EXTEND. This does not apply to VSAM sequential files. File status 0
is returned.
  	  	7 	For a CLOSE statement with the NO REWIND,
REEL/UNIT, or FOR REMOVAL phrase or for an OPEN statement with the NO
REWIND phrase, the referenced file was on a non-reel/unit medium.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Philosphical" question on "succesful" file status testing

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-01T07:39:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1p3c52dgv6fcd5qsrs457b54v3ovu8rpd3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<M_M4g.173857$zy2.141605@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sat, 29 Apr 2006 17:02:36 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>The question is (then) what happens if the implementation (as an extension, new 
>implementor defined "0x" or the Standard) introduces a new "0?" successful 
>status code.

A trouble with this is that just because a status is "good" doesn't
mean we want to continue as is.

Nor does it mean that just because a status is "bad" doesn't mean we
want to abend.

If the vendor wants to implement a new status code because it comes up
with a new file type with different feedback, it might not be a good
idea to assume that the old status checking is applicable.
```

---

- **References:** `<1146124496.436402.108270@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1146166467.365696.128010@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1146315339.149350.221370@j73g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1146324067.476214.230680@j73g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1146553823.072798.208440@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <1146553917.724747.44140@j33g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Here is the code:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. INSTRM01.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  WS-IN  PIC X(05).
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
PARA-A.
    DISPLAY 'START EXECUTION.'.
    PERFORM PARA-B UNTIL WS-IN = 'XX'.
    DISPLAY 'STOP EXECUTION.'.
    STOP RUN.
PARA-B.
    ACCEPT WS-IN.
    IF WS-IN = 'XX'
       CONTINUE
    ELSE
       DISPLAY 'INSTREAM DATA ' WS-IN
    END-IF.

Any updates?
```

```
arrbee wrote:
> Here is the code:
>

> 01  WS-IN  PIC X(05).

Depending on your environment, you might want to put "value spaces" 
after the PIC clause.  If you're really unlucky, junk in this field 
might cause this test...

> PROCEDURE DIVISION.
> PARA-A.
>     DISPLAY 'START EXECUTION.'.
>     PERFORM PARA-B UNTIL WS-IN = 'XX'.

...to be true before PARA-B is executed at all.

[remaining code snipped]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prodcuing an output file only on Friday?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Jeff Campbell <n8wxs@arrl.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-01T16:58:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1146524009_1343@sp6iad.superfeed.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4bikroF109h7cU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1145604671.952859.159440@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <124h4ca8bpmba9b@corp.supernews.com> <444D518F.EA52E1D4@mts.net> <4b58o5Fv56roU1@individual.net> <444E3CC6.6252500@mts.net> <4bbl2tF1004ivU1@individual.net> <44513A41.EEB25EB9@mts.net> <4bf6f4F114a0pU1@individual.net> <1146264261_5247@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4bikroF109h7cU1@individual.net>`

```
One a different topic...

Recently, I needed to create, write and read some values stored in an NT registry.
In the past I've just called the API routines. This time I decided to try
your 8-) component approach, using a PowerCOBOL ActiveX project. Specifically,
I implemented it with all of the input/output as properties. None of the
method invocations have any arguments. Here is a snippet of code using the
resulting component:

      move HKEY_CURRENT_USER    to "key"        OF registry1
      move sub-key              to "subkey"     OF registry1
      move value-name-frequency to "value-name" OF registry1
      move REG_DWORD            to "value-type" OF registry1

      INVOKE registry1 "read-value"

      move "status" OF registry1 to ReturnValue

      if error_success then
          move "value-integer" OF registry1 to frequency
          move frequency to "Text" OF freqbox
      end-if

I really like the idea of each invokable method not having arguments. While
some properties are unique to a given method, most turned out to be common to
several methods, like the value's name and type above.

So, thanks for a good idea!

Jeff


Top Post. No more.

Pete Dashwood wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Prodcuing an output file only on Friday?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-05-02T23:32:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4boub9F125pkeU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1145604671.952859.159440@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <124h4ca8bpmba9b@corp.supernews.com> <444D518F.EA52E1D4@mts.net> <4b58o5Fv56roU1@individual.net> <444E3CC6.6252500@mts.net> <4bbl2tF1004ivU1@individual.net> <44513A41.EEB25EB9@mts.net> <4bf6f4F114a0pU1@individual.net> <1146264261_5247@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4bikroF109h7cU1@individual.net> <1146524009_1343@sp6iad.superfeed.net>`

```
Thanks for that, Jeff.

Your post made my day :-)

I can't believe anyone in CLC has actually taken on board what I've been 
saying for some years now, but the fact that someone even tried it makes me 
think the posts were not wasted.

I'm really glad it worked and you are happy with it.

The nice thing (as I'm sure you realize) is that you will never need to 
maintain this solution. There will be no interface changes on existing 
calls, and any other calls you add will not cause regression testing of the 
existing code, because they cannot possibly affect it.

I see you have referenced the properties using PowerCOBOL and MOVE. This is 
equivalent to using GET- and SET- on a COM (OLE) component. (This is how OO 
NetCOBOL would allow you to reference an automation server like Excel, MS 
Word, or whatever..)

I like the approach of GETing and SETing Properties, rather than passing 
parameters at run time, for the very reason you have found here. Methods of 
the object will not change and the interface to them will not change. It 
does what it does. (I have had great difficulty in getting that concept 
across in this forum.)

Good job!

Pete
```

```
I've never seen mention of this specific type of coding, but I do find it
interesting.  Would the following Java be similar?

registry1.setKey(HKEY_CURRENT_USER);
registry1.setSubKey(subKey);
registry1.setValueName(valueNameFrequency);
registry1.setValueType(REG_DWORD);
registry1.read_value();
if (registry1.getStatus() == success) {
    frequency = registry1.getValueInteger();
    freqBox.setText(frequency);
}

>      if error_success then
>          move "value-integer" OF registry1 to frequency
>          move frequency to "Text" OF freqbox
>      end-if

Actually, I'm rather fond of the following Ruby (well, I think it's valid
Ruby...)

registry1.key = HKEY_CURRENT_USER
registry1.subkey = sub_key
registry1.value_name = value_name_frequency
registry1.value_type = REG_DWORD
registry1.read_value()
if registry1.status == success
    frequency = registry1.valueInteger
    freqBox.Text =frequency
end

Believe it or not, I am another who prefers this to passing a huge number of
parameters, ie:
invoke "read-value" using HKEY_CURRENT_USER, sub-key, value-name-frequency,
REG_DWORD

I am curious as to what the factory1 class looks like.  Is the ["attribute"
OF object] language part of COBOL 2002?  I've never seen it.  I'm not even
sure if "attribute" is the correct name for it.  In Ruby that's what it's
called, but in C# it's called a property.  Anyway, where might I find more
details on that feature?

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Jeff Campbell <n8wxs@arrl.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-04T20:54:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1146797331_2223@sp6iad.superfeed.net>`

- **References:** `<1145604671.952859.159440@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <124h4ca8bpmba9b@corp.supernews.com> <444D518F.EA52E1D4@mts.net> <4b58o5Fv56roU1@individual.net> <444E3CC6.6252500@mts.net> <4bbl2tF1004ivU1@individual.net> <44513A41.EEB25EB9@mts.net> <4bf6f4F114a0pU1@individual.net> <1146264261_5247@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4bikroF109h7cU1@individual.net> <1146524009_1343@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4boub9F125pkeU1@individual.net> <4bplq7F12opfpU1@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> I've never seen mention of this specific type of coding, but I do find it
> interesting.  Would the following Java be similar?
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Functionally, I think what you wrote is equivalent. I don't know how one references
properties in java.

     registry1.setKey(HKEY_CURRENT_USER);

looks like a function call to me. 8-)

I would have expected a property assignment to look like:

     "registry1.Key = HKEY_CURRENT_USER;"

In PowerCOBOL, the GET and SET methods of the object are hidden. PowerCOBOL provides
the 'translation' between assignment and reference. So, one MOVEs values to/from
properties. Properties can be used in other contexts. For example:

    add "frequency" of registry1 to some-variable

    If "frequency" of registry1 is greater than zero then...

> Actually, I'm rather fond of the following Ruby (well, I think it's valid
> Ruby...)

Your Ruby example looks like what I would have expected the java example
to be.

> registry1.key = HKEY_CURRENT_USER
> registry1.subkey = sub_key
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> details on that feature?

There is not a separate factory1 class. Rather, there is a single class with a
factory object and a PowerCOBOL form object. PowerCOBOL does not provide access
to factory object's methods or properties. The user defined properties and methods
of the form object are managed by these hidden methods. All of the user defined
methods and properties are methods and properties of the form object.

I don't know anything about what the standards say, i.e., COBOL 2002. 8-)

If I had created a registry object using Fujitsu COBOL instead of PowerCOBOL,
the factory object would contain all of the 'public' (class) properties. I could
then have used the GET and SET methodology that Pete mentioned earlier.
I think setting the factory frequency property would look like:

    invoke registry1 "SET-frequency" using frequency

> 
> Frank
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

Jeff

Jeff Campbell
n8wxs!arrl.net

[rest snipped]
```

```
Jeff Campbell<n8wxs@arrl.net> 05/04/06 8:54 PM >>>
>Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>> I've never seen mention of this specific type of coding, but I do find
it
>> interesting.  Would the following Java be similar?
>> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Functionally, I think what you wrote is equivalent. I don't know how one
references
>properties in java.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     "registry1.Key = HKEY_CURRENT_USER;"

I don't believe that Java has properties, as such.  You must explicitly
define "getter" and "setter" methods, i.e.

class MyClass {
    private string key;
    public void setKey(string key) {
        this.key = key;
    }
    public StatusClass getStatus() {
        return new StatusClass(subKey);
    }
}

If Java *does* have actual "properties" in the same manner as C# and,
apparently, COBOL, I'd be interested to know about them.

>In PowerCOBOL, the GET and SET methods of the object are hidden. PowerCOBOL
provides
>the 'translation' between assignment and reference. So, one MOVEs values
to/from
>properties. Properties can be used in other contexts. For example:
>
>    add "frequency" of registry1 to some-variable
>
>    If "frequency" of registry1 is greater than zero then...

Are the quotes around the property name required?  I find them a bit
distracting...

>> Actually, I'm rather fond of the following Ruby (well, I think it's
valid
>> Ruby...)
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Believe it or not, I am another who prefers this to passing a huge number
of
>> parameters, ie:
>> invoke "read-value" using HKEY_CURRENT_USER, sub-key,
value-name-frequency,
>> REG_DWORD
>> 
>> I am curious as to what the factory1 class looks like.  Is the
["attribute"
>> OF object] language part of COBOL 2002?  I've never seen it.  I'm not
even
>> sure if "attribute" is the correct name for it.  In Ruby that's what
it's
>> called, but in C# it's called a property.  Anyway, where might I find
more
>> details on that feature?
>
>There is not a separate factory1 class. Rather, there is a single class
with a
>factory object and a PowerCOBOL form object. PowerCOBOL does not provide
access
>to factory object's methods or properties. The user defined properties and
methods
>of the form object are managed by these hidden methods. All of the user
defined
>methods and properties are methods and properties of the form object.
>
>I don't know anything about what the standards say, i.e., COBOL 2002. 8-)

I took a harder look at the COBOL 2002 standard and it looks like there are
two ways that one can define properties; explicit and implicit  For
example:

******************************
* <properties: explicit definition>
******************************
class-id. my-class.
data division.
working-storage section.
01  my-val                  pic x(10).

method-id. get property my-val.
data division.
linkage section.
01  ls-my-val               pic x(10).
procedure division returning ls-my-val
    move my-val to ls-my-val
    exit method.
end method.

method-id. set property my-val.
data division.
linkage section.
01  ls-my-val               pic x(10).
procedure division using ls-my-val
    move ls-my-val to my-val
    exit method.
end method.

end-class.
******************************
* <properties: implicit definition>
******************************
class-id. my-class.
data division.
working-storage section.
01  my-val                  pic x(10) is property.
end-class.
******************************
* <properties: testing>
******************************
program-id. main.
data division.
local-storage section.
01  my-object               object reference my-class.
procedure division.
    set my-object to my-class::new
    move "testing" to "my-val" of my-object
    display "my-val" of my-object
    exit program.
end program main.

Having no way to compile this I cannot validate it, but I'm guessing it's
pretty close.

If anyone's curious, I believe this is how you'd do it in Ruby:

#######################
# <attributes: explicit definition>
#######################
class MyClass
    def my_val   # this is the "getter" 
        @my_val
    end

    def my_val=(new_val)  # this is the "setter"
        @my_val = new_val
    end
end
        
#######################
# <attributes: implicit definition>
#######################
class MyClass
    attr :my_val  # defines both "getter" and "setter"
#  or
    attr_reader :my_val  # defines only "getter"
    attr_writer :my_val # defines only "setter"
end
#######################
# <attributes: testing>
#######################
my_object = MyClass.new
my_object.my_val = "testing"
puts my_object.my_val

So in any case it looks like COBOL is ahead of Java, here!  Still, to be
honest, I prefer the Ruby version the most...

Frank




--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<4c1c37F12s317U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<1145604671.952859.159440@v46g2000cwv.googlegroups.com> <124h4ca8bpmba9b@corp.supernews.com> <444D518F.EA52E1D4@mts.net> <4b58o5Fv56roU1@individual.net> <444E3CC6.6252500@mts.net> <4bbl2tF1004ivU1@individual.net> <44513A41.EEB25EB9@mts.net> <4bf6f4F114a0pU1@individual.net> <1146264261_5247@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4bikroF109h7cU1@individual.net> <1146524009_1343@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4boub9F125pkeU1@individual.net> <4bplq7F12opfpU1@individual.net> <1146797331_2223@sp6iad.superfeed.net> <4c1c37F12s317U1@individual.net>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> Jeff Campbell<n8wxs@arrl.net> 05/04/06 8:54 PM >>>

Careful when generalizing Frank. There's COBOL 2002 and then there's 
IBM, Fujitsu and Micro Focus, and lurking in the background Hitachi - 
what they have each independently implemented - so there maybe minor 
conflicts.

I'm not aware of any clear definition, but I think the words 
'Attribute', 'Property' or 'Style' are interchangeable in a OOP context. 
Probably makes sense to stick with 'Property' as that's the word used 
with GET or SET.

Java Properties - Yes - take a look at :-

http://mindprod.com/jgloss/properties.html

If Oliver disagrees with what Roedy Green has above, be sure he will 
chip in :-).

Very likely that Fujitsu and Micro Focus handle GET and SET in a 
slightly different fashion. M/F version - see following :-

http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/oppubb.htm

There are other issues with compilers - Pete is adamant about doing 
dynamic binding when OO is involved, (as opposed to static). No choice 
in M/F - as soon as the compiler spots OO jargon you are automatically 
into Dynamic Binding using the Shared Runtime System.

***********
The reference to MOVing fields made me smile. When you are first boning 
up on OO you come up against the $10 word ENCAPSULATION. A class does a 
particular thing for a given object(record/fields) which is accessed by 
methods - it's hidden from you the invoking developer - you invoke using 
specific methods which may/may not have parameters and returning values.

M/F on-line manual :-

"Encapsulation in OO languages is the inclusion within an object of 
everything it needs to function: its data and the implementation of its 
methods. Other objects can use it as long as they adhere to the object's 
interface; they do not need to know anything about the way the object 
holds and manipulates its data. Encapsulation helps to preserve the 
integrity of data."

The 'traditional' way to handle files/DBs is to access data from the 
data-repository and send it back to an instance of, say, Customer-Record 
Class. Given there are GET or SET METHODS - then you the developer, 
(because you are playing it 'blind'),  must be provided with those 
method names and any sending/receiving parameters.

Let's assume, (an international software house), your file/DB access and 
resulting record-classes are coded by your office in the UK which 
provides you with a DLL, plus documentation on methods for GET and SET - 
that's *assuming* they code GET/SET methods.

Then somebody in US chooses to code :-

move Name    of CustomerRecordClass to my-Name
move City    of CustomerRecordClass to my-City
move ZipCode of CustomerRecordClass to my-Zipcode

Funny. The above CustomerRecordClass was written in UK - so chances are 
they could possibly have 'Town' instead of 'City' and PostalCode instead 
of 'Zipcode'. Given Encapsulation = no source available - How do you 
figure out those MOVEs  ???  :-)

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:l1P6g.128538$WI1.52140@pd7tw2no...
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>> Jeff Campbell<n8wxs@arrl.net> 05/04/06 8:54 PM >>>
>
<snip>>
> Let's assume, (an international software house), your file/DB access and 
> resulting record-classes are coded by your office in the UK which provides 
> you with a DLL, plus documentation on methods for GET and SET - that's 
> *assuming* they code GET/SET methods.
>
The documentation is a help file that documents the Methods, Properties, and 
Events. Anyone developing components for other people to use would be crazy 
NOT to provide such documentation (no one would buy it if you were selling 
it) and the fact that this is being imposed from Head Office doesn't mean 
they don't have to document it. If I were required to use it I would simply 
ask politely for the documentation... In the meantime, I'd make a start on 
it using the approach described below.

 But such a scenario is extremely unlikely. People who work in this 
environment KNOW that components need to be documented, especially as source 
code will not be provided. I think I have mentioned here on several 
occasions that writing the help file for a component can sometimes take more 
time than the development of the component itself. The Help file provides a 
quick reference via hyperlinks to whatever you are interested in, and it 
also has 'background' as to why certain approaches were adopted.

> Then somebody in US chooses to code :-
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Should have checked the documentation before using the Properties... If it 
was being developed for international use it is likely that different sets 
of properties would become visible, depending on the country. Set the 
country code and suddenly a new set of properties is enabled...

> Funny. The above CustomerRecordClass was written in UK - so chances are 
> they could possibly have 'Town' instead of 'City' and PostalCode instead 
> of 'Zipcode'. Given Encapsulation = no source available - How do you 
> figure out those MOVEs  ???  :-)

Easily. You apply an Object Browser to it and it SHOWS you the Interfaces, 
Methods and Properties supported by the component.

Anybody working with OO components will make frequent use of such a tool. A 
very entertaining afternoon can be spent (and I've done this a couple of 
times since I acquired Visual Studio, which comes with an absolutely 
excellent Object Browser) just looking at all the ActiveX and COM components 
which are provided with Windows...:-) There is amazing stuff sitting right 
there on your computer, free for you to use, all you need to do is find it. 
Object Browsing is a bit like treasure hunting... :-)

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:l1P6g.128538$WI1.52140@pd7tw2no...
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Oi ! Who said anything about components ? (I knew you would comment 
anyway - that's why I didn't mention your, "Yer don't need source").  Or 
are you now extending the component family to include discrete written 
classes for an installation's own DB Tables and COBOL files, plus of 
course those record classes.

I agree that you have to have documentation with your class/component if 
   the source is to be hidden from the serfs. (Reference to Michael M's 
'expensive' people producing those DB copy files and the 'also rans', 
the cheap labour, incorporate them into their programs without really 
needing to know which way is up - and of course - compile. Uggh).

But bear in mind I wrote they don't necessarily produce GETs/SETs - YOU 
do, but that doesn't mean its the only way. So MOVE x of anObject is a 
perfectly valid alternative. Think a name and address block - why a 
series of -

getName
getAddressLine1
getAddressLine2
getCity etc..., or

MOVE name of xxxx to my-name
MOVE AddressLine1 to of xxx to my-AddressLine1 etc.

shortcut the damn thing -

MOVE CustomerAddressData of xxx to my-AddressData


Now think back on OO before you got the component buzz - so called total 
ENCAPSULATION  is theoretically possible and desirable. But it is not 
the whole story. Given a Treeview, for example, that I want to change 
icons from Red to Green when 'actioned' - chances are the supporting 
classes available may not appear to have the feature to do this - but 
the inheritee has it available from its inheritor class - and it is as 
easy as pie to override providing YOU HAVE THE SOURCE.

Actually Treeview is a fascinating control that you can embrace all 
sorts of changes in, colouring horizontal and vertical hierachial lines 
, hang combo droplists on to Labels, substitute Checkboxes On/Off icons 
instead of folders or 'balls' etc. Nobody is likely to want the complete 
set but given access to SOURCE such features are fairly easily incorporated.

Let's lump three topics together -

(a) Frank commenting about using tools like EXCEL etc., followed by 
Peter saying he got the shove, (the nice term over here is "We had to 
let him go"), because as DP he suggested it might be desirable if he had 
a watching brief over what individual end-users were doing with these 
tools. Indirectly this topic ties in with (c) below to some extent.

Practical horror story on above. Some smart cookie in downtown real 
estate about 15 years back decides he'll set up a DB, (can't recall what 
the tool was). He had already started on it keying in quite a bit of 
data, at lest 2,000 properties. Had me and a non-IT guy in to see if we 
were interested in getting involved. Questions raised, observations made.

Then we have a second meeting. Tell us he has already incorporated some 
changes in the DB - "Took some time",  he said. "I had to re-design the 
tables and enter the data all over again". Ouch, what about exporting 
and importing back to the DB - had I suggested it he would have thought 
exporting/importing had something to do with Canada's trade deficit.

(b) Michael M. points out, and a correct observation, you use your 
experienced expensive folks under the DBA to produce SQL copy files to 
be used by serfs - short answer to that one would be to be include the 
routines in OO classes - but how many here actually have access to an OO 
compiler.

(Actually the 27-year old geek that lives next door to me - moves out in 
June - is a 6' 6" turd with not very much between the ears, colourless 
and humourless, and fits into Michael's scenario. As he told me with a 
painful look, he finds his job "Stressful and challenging". He knows two 
words 'Oracle' and 'Java'. Well he knows three words - 'DBA' - he trots 
off to see him for one of those wonderful copy files as necessary. 'DB2' 
is not even in his vocabulary as a passing thought).

(c) Then one of your latest - and the one which really troubles me - Hey 
! I'll be stone dead before it has any real impact, (although it is 
partially happening in (b) above). You'll be in your dotage hoping some 
kind soul wheels you in your chair down to the beach occasionally.

You wrote eventually we finish up with a grand Lego set - folks just 
plugging bits together. Well the toy Lego you can see and kick - but not 
your Lego components. Inevitably it will finish up with a few 
powerhouses producing these computer Lego sets. Just like commerce has 
superseded borders by going international - we will have these tool 
powerhouses calling the shots. It will of course work just great, (at a 
price when they have shut off much of the junior competition).

Here we are in 2006 - what happens in 2056 ? If the production of 
computer Lego tools is constrained to a few powerhouses, what happens 
when some inquisitive kid called 'Steve' or 'Bill' thinks he'd like to 
get into this game - where does he start, having to face the Cabal set 
up by the current producers ?

Fortunately some humans are inquisitive and imaginative and will break 
through the firewall created by those powerhouses. But it will be tough. 
Meanwhile the silent majority will go along with what is, not giving a 
thought to new approaches until such time as they happen; then there's 
the timelag before they are proven and accepted.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:7kW6g.130268$P01.86836@pd7tw3no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
> Oi ! Who said anything about components ?

You did.
There is more than one type of component. An OO Class packaged as a .DLL 
(which is what you described) only needs to be registered to be browseable 
with an Object Browser. (There are probably Browsers that don't even require 
it to be registered.)
If it happens to have been wrapped as a COM DLL it is then a component; if 
it includes certain interfaces it is an ActiveX component.


(I knew you would comment
> anyway - that's why I didn't mention your, "Yer don't need source").  Or 
> are you now extending the component family to include discrete written 
> classes for an installation's own DB Tables and COBOL files, plus of 
> course those record classes.
>
I don't need to extend the component family. See above. OO Classes, packaged 
as .DLLs are one type of component.

> I agree that you have to have documentation with your class/component if 
> the source is to be hidden from the serfs. (Reference to Michael M's 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This has been a very effective approach for some decades now.

> But bear in mind I wrote they don't necessarily produce GETs/SETs - YOU 
> do, but that doesn't mean its the only way. So MOVE x of anObject is a 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Makes no difference. It is just a different property. Move x of an Object is 
just another form of GET/SET. (I thought that was covered by the previous 
poster...)

>
> Now think back on OO before you got the component buzz - so called total 
> ENCAPSULATION  is theoretically possible and desirable. But it is not the 
> whole story.

Who suggested that it was? You'd normally add polymorphism, reuse, 
inheritance and interfacing before you'd even suggest that that MIGHT be the 
whole story...


> Given a Treeview, for example, that I want to change icons from Red to 
> Green when 'actioned' - chances are the supporting classes available may 
> not appear to have the feature to do this - but the inheritee has it 
> available from its inheritor class - and it is as easy as pie to override 
> providing YOU HAVE THE SOURCE.

It's as easy as pie to obtain this behaviour, whether you have the source or 
not, if it is written properly.

> Actually Treeview is a fascinating control that you can embrace all sorts 
> of changes in, colouring horizontal and vertical hierachial lines , hang 
> combo droplists on to Labels, substitute Checkboxes On/Off icons instead 
> of folders or 'balls' etc. Nobody is likely to want the complete set but 
> given access to SOURCE such features are fairly easily incorporated.
There are many treeview controls, not just one. All the ones I've come 
across utilise a node collection for the data and allow the facilities you 
describe by a combination of Properties and Methods. You don't need source 
to use them. Having it is no bad thing if you want to work at such a low 
level but there is nothing to be gained by doing so if the code was well 
written in the first place. Why would anyone rewrite well written working 
code?

You don't need access to source if it is written properly and exposes the 
right methods and properties. I use several treeviews and do  not have the 
source of them. Actually any one of them would meet my needs; I have a 
number because I was driven by curiosity to see if any one of them was 
better then the others :-)

I also have a MUCH more complex scheduling grid component that I have no 
source for (It has over 200 methods) and produces text in a range of colours 
determined by a property.

Source is definitely NOT a requirement if you are using objects.
>
> Let's lump three topics together -
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler.

Or you write general purpose access modules (as we did in the 70s) that are 
cappable of carrying out ANY action on a given table or tables and CALL 
them. The same effect is achieved today (much more efficiently) by stored 
procedures which are tailored to a given action against a given database or 
table. These are under control of the DBMS and can run asynchronously. The 
application only needs to trigger them. ESQL is kind of 'quaint' in this 
environment... I haven't written any for years... Stored [procedures are an 
extension of OO philosophy really, because you are storing the methods to 
handle data, along with the data that will be handled.  It took me a while 
to warm to this idea, but once I saw some experts working with it I was 
sold.
>
> (Actually the 27-year old geek that lives next door to me - moves out in 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> kind soul wheels you in your chair down to the beach occasionally.

Nope, that ain't gonna happen... I'm gonna buy one of those super groovy 
electric chairs, tweak it a bit and drive myself to the beach when they pull 
my license... :-)

>
> You wrote eventually we finish up with a grand Lego set - folks just 
> plugging bits together.

Yes, I believe we will end up with end users configuring software to do what 
they want. They'll do this by graphically 'assembling Lego' where each block 
will represent a configurable Business process. (under the covers there will 
be many such blocks, but the user may not be aware of them). And they'll 
probably refer to the process as 'programming', in memory of the ancient art 
that was so important in the latter half of the twentieth century...

Have you seen Visual Studio :-)? You can  write code (in any of C#, VB, C++ 
(in fact you can mix them if you want because the source is unimportant), 
and a few Scripting languages), create a component from it in minutes, join 
that to other components using drag and drop, then deploy the whole lot as 
either a distributed application with Remote Procedure Calls  for a network, 
ASP pages for a web server, or a static Windows desktop application (with NO 
runtime fees for any of it...)

It is a very long way from coding COBOL, even if you decide to write a code 
based solution, rather than using components.

> Well the toy Lego you can see and kick - but not your Lego components.

Why would you want to kick either of them?

>Inevitably it will finish up with a few powerhouses producing these 
>computer Lego sets.

Why not? WE tolerate it with motor cars...

 Just like commerce has
> superseded borders by going international - we will have these tool 
> powerhouses calling the shots. It will of course work just great, (at a 
> price when they have shut off much of the junior competition).
>
Yes, I think that is a possible scenario..
.
> Here we are in 2006 - what happens in 2056 ? If the production of computer 
> Lego tools is constrained to a few powerhouses, what happens when some 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
By 2056 there will be no requirement for manual programming of computers. 
Computers will simply do whatever you ask them to do,  a la 'Hal'.

There are some major breakthroughs in the pipeline already, and, 
statistically, we can expect at least one iconoclastic development that will 
change the whole game. The powerhouses you describe will probably still be 
around, but it is very unlikely they will be building Lego sets for 
commercial computer programming.

> Fortunately some humans are inquisitive and imaginative and will break 
> through the firewall created by those powerhouses.

Just like the imaginative innovative people who have broken through the 
stranglehold of GM, Ford, et al in the motor industry? Around 30 brands 
worldwide have it sewn up. How long is it since we saw a new successful 
marque of autmobile? OK... DeLorean :-).  Is anybody suffering? I don't see 
major protest marches because Toyota or BMW cannot be effectively competed 
with in their markets.

Instead, while the rest of the world drives a Ford and dreams of a BMW the 
Germans drive a BMW and dream of a Mercedes...:-)

>But it will be tough. Meanwhile the silent majority will go along with what 
>is, not giving a thought to new approaches until such time as they happen; 
>then there's the timelag before they are proven and accepted.

I thought your point was they would not happen... guess I lost this 
somewhere. You appeared to be saying no bright young people would get a look 
in, and now you are saying innovation will happen anyway.

So, if the big cartels are innovating, they'll probably be employing bright 
young people and paying them more than if they tried to start up their own 
companies...Doesn't that mean that the rest of us are right to simply 'go 
along with what is'?

I'm not sure what you are actually getting at here.

But I really don't think it matters. Computer programming is a dying art. 
Configurable packages will take care of it within the next 10 - 20 years; 
you won't have to wait for 2056...

In house IT will be relegated to care of the network, configuring servers, 
upgrading the OS, help desk and training, and installing new software. Users 
will put their own applications together using much better tools than are 
currently available. Core business processes will be run by SAP, Siebel, 
ORACLE and a couple of others that are likely to emerge. EDI and B2B will 
grow and the web will pervade our lives and businesses to an extent not even 
seen yet. Wireless internet will mean we are always on line and this alone 
will change the way in which people do business.

The last thing you need to worry about is how I see components being glued 
together...:-)

Pete.
```

```
James J. Gavan<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> 05/05/06 3:00 PM >>>
>
>Careful when generalizing Frank. There's COBOL 2002 and then there's 
>IBM, Fujitsu and Micro Focus, and lurking in the background Hitachi - 
>what they have each independently implemented - so there maybe minor 
>conflicts.

I'm not sure to what you are referring, since you did not quote me, but I
got my information from "ISO/IEC 1989:200x WD 1.5", which appears to be the
working draft for the next COBOL standard.  Actually, at the time I wrote
the message I thought I was looking at "ISO/IEC 1989:2002", which is the
COBOL 2002 standard.  I must have that one at home and the new one here at
work.  Humm...  Ah well.  Does anyone know offhand if the property
clause/phrase is defined in the current standard?

>I'm not aware of any clear definition, but I think the words 
>'Attribute', 'Property' or 'Style' are interchangeable in a OOP context. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>chip in :-).

I do not believe that that type of "property" is the same as what we are
referring to.  We were talking about object (class or instance) properties. 
That page gives a small blurb about them
(http://mindprod.com/jgloss/properties.html#JAVABEAN):
"JavaBean Properties
Property has a second meaning. In JavaBeans, components have associated
persistent objects. You can modify various fields in those objects to
configure them. The accessible fields of a JavaBean are called properties.
They are accessible via public get/set methods. There need not be an actual
field, just the get/set methods that simulate one. These are a completely
separate mechanism. "

It's the "JavaBean" properties that I was referring to, where explicit SET
and GET methods are required.

>Very likely that Fujitsu and Micro Focus handle GET and SET in a 
>slightly different fashion. M/F version - see following :-
>
>http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/oppu
bb.htm 

>There are other issues with compilers - Pete is adamant about doing 
>dynamic binding when OO is involved, (as opposed to static). No choice 
>in M/F - as soon as the compiler spots OO jargon you are automatically 
>into Dynamic Binding using the Shared Runtime System.

I'm not sure what you're getting at here, but I have to agree that dynamic
binding is the only way to go here.  Or for any other situation, for that
matter.  All that static binding gains you is a bit of speed and a whole lot
of pain when you have to relink everything!  :-)

>***********
>The reference to MOVing fields made me smile. When you are first boning 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>specific methods which may/may not have parameters and returning values.

Well, properties are, in fact, methods.  They are just quite often methods
that simply get or set an encapsulated variable.  But they don't *need* to
do this.  There is nothing to say that the "interestRate" property didn't
start as being set directly from a variable called "interestRate", but has
now been changed to be a table lookup or a calculation or something.  So
again, I'm not sure what point you're trying to make.

>M/F on-line manual :-
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>integrity of data."

Yes...

>The 'traditional' way to handle files/DBs is to access data from the 
>data-repository and send it back to an instance of, say, Customer-Record 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>figure out those MOVEs  ???  :-)

But City of CustomerRecordClass is a *property* of CustomerRecordClass, not
a variable of said class.  I'm not sure on how public/private etc. work in
OOCOBOL, but it seems to me that all class variables should be private and
only properties and methods should be public.  So if City is a "get
property" of CustomerRecordClass then obviously(?!) it was intentionally
made available as such.

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

```
"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:4c9oubF15560qU1@individual.net...
> James J. Gavan<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> 05/05/06 3:00 PM >>>
>>
<snip>
> I do not believe that that type of "property" is the same as what we are
> referring to.  We were talking about object (class or instance) 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> and GET methods are required.

Your distinction is correct as far as Java goes, but any OO Class can have 
Properties and define GET/SET Methods to manipulate them. Certainly, they 
are definable in Fujitsu OO COBOL and PowerCOBOL (I use them all the time). 
The Fujitsu OLE/COM interface automatically allows GET/SET for any property 
of the COM/ActiveX component being accessed, whether those methods are 
actually defined within the component or not...
>
>>Very likely that Fujitsu and Micro Focus handle GET and SET in a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>dynamic binding when OO is involved, (as opposed to static).
No I'm not.

I'm only adamant about not being adamant :-)

As it happens, I always opt for late binding, but that's just me and I don't 
think people who don't are wrong. There are occasions where you might be 
glad to use static binding (prototyping for example), I just don't 
personally do that.


>No choice
>>in M/F - as soon as the compiler spots OO jargon you are automatically
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yeah, what did the Romans ever do for us...nothing right?
REG:
Yeah. All right, Stan. Don't labour the point. And what have they ever given 
us in return?!
XERXES:
The aqueduct?
REG:
What?
XERXES:
The aqueduct.
REG:
Oh. Yeah, yeah. They did give us that. Uh, that's true. Yeah.
COMMANDO #3:
And the sanitation.
LORETTA:
Oh, yeah, the sanitation, Reg. Remember what the city used to be like?
REG:
Yeah. All right. I'll grant you the aqueduct and the sanitation are two 
things that the Romans have done.
MATTHIAS:
And the roads.
REG:
Well, yeah. Obviously the roads. I mean, the roads go without saying, don't 
they? But apart from the sanitation, the aqueduct, and the roads--
COMMANDO:
Irrigation.
XERXES:
Medicine.
COMMANDOS:
Huh? Heh? Huh...
COMMANDO #2:
Education.
COMMANDOS:
Ohh...
REG:
Yeah, yeah. All right. Fair enough.
COMMANDO #1:
And the wine.
COMMANDOS:
Oh, yes. Yeah...
FRANCIS:
Yeah. Yeah, that's something we'd really miss, Reg, if the Romans left. Huh.
COMMANDO:
Public baths.
LORETTA:
And it's safe to walk in the streets at night now, Reg.
FRANCIS:
Yeah, they certainly know how to keep order. Let's face it. They're the only 
ones who could in a place like this.
COMMANDOS:
Hehh, heh. Heh heh heh heh heh heh heh.
REG:
All right, but apart from the sanitation, the medicine, education, wine, 
public order, irrigation, roads, a fresh water system, and public health, 
what have the Romans ever done for us?
XERXES:
Brought peace.
REG:
Oh. Peace? Shut up!

So what's early binding (static linking if you like) ever done for us?

Apart from protoyping, compliance checking of all method invocations at 
compile time,  better performance at run time, phased development without 
having to write stubs as you would for late binding, multiple inheritance, 
and nested object methods...nothing, right? :-)

As I have remarked here before, it is wise to hesitate before using the 
phrase, 'the only way'... :-)

I don't use early binding myself, but I'm not 'adamant' about it and I would 
NOT rule out using it if circumstances warranted it.

>>***********
>>The reference to MOVing fields made me smile. When you are first boning
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Well, properties are, in fact, methods.

HUH?!!!  If you are saying that a method which manipulates a property is a 
method, well OK...

> They are just quite often methods
> that simply get or set an encapsulated variable.

Ah, you ARE saying that... :-) But even if the method is implicit, it still 
isn't a Property. I always think of Properties as being data (even though 
they may occasionally occupy zero space), and methods as 'behaviour'.

I think the confusion here may be between class variables (whch would be 
PRIVATE in some languages), and PROPERTIES which are usually PUBLIC. The 
same applies in COBOL; Local-storage variables are private unless you 
declare them as PROPERTY. A method which is used to manipulate a private 
Class variable is still a method. It may return or set a Property.

>But they don't *need* to
> do this.  There is nothing to say that the "interestRate" property didn't
> start as being set directly from a variable called "interestRate", but has
> now been changed to be a table lookup or a calculation or something.

No, I agree with that. But "interestRate", as soon as it is made visible, is 
a property, not a method. Even if it was calculated out of thin air and 
returned by the GET-interestRate Method, what is returned is a copy of it in 
storage. It is storage. It can only be a class variable or a Property. Which 
one it actually is, is decided by whether it is Public or Private. If you 
can "see" it, it is a Property.

> So
> again, I'm not sure what point you're trying to make.
>

I'm not sure what point either of you are trying to make, but it's fun isn't 
it :-)?


>>M/F on-line manual :-
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Or you could look at them with an Object Browser if they are explicit 
Methods or interfaces.


>>Let's assume, (an international software house), your file/DB access and
>>resulting record-classes are coded by your office in the UK which
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> Jeff Campbell<n8wxs@arrl.net> 05/04/06 8:54 PM >>>
>> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> apparently, COBOL, I'd be interested to know about them.

Don't know. Not doing java (yet.) 8-)

> 
>> In PowerCOBOL, the GET and SET methods of the object are hidden. PowerCOBOL
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

No, they are not. The quotes are necessary for property names that happen
to be PowerCOBOL reserved words. I'm just in the habit of quoting.

>>> Actually, I'm rather fond of the following Ruby (well, I think it's
> valid
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
> pretty close.

I will check the above with the COBOL97 to which I have access. The Fujitsu
COBOL97 users guide I have shows both implicit and explicit property method
definitions that are close to your examples. The chief difference is that the
property variables  are declared in the factory data division. The class doesn't
have a data division The language reference manual shows:

    Class Definition
            [IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.]
                CLASS-ID. class-name-1 [AS literal-1]
                    [INHERITS {class-name-2}...].
            [ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.]
            [factory definition.]
            [object definition.]
                END CLASS class-name-1.

I have not looked at the COBOL 2002 standard, so maybe class definitions
now have a data division as well.

> 
> If anyone's curious, I believe this is how you'd do it in Ruby:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> honest, I prefer the Ruby version the most...

I went to rubycentral.com and read the online manual. Very interesting. 8-)

> 
> Frank
```
