###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-05-27T03:03:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> One justification for NOT allowing colons in PICTURE character-strings has 
> for some time been that you could code
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> modification approach.

I don't.

I think it is appalling. Why would I use an INSPECT when I can use a MOVE?

People who say refmodding is difficult are usually the same people who ban 
PERFORM...VARYING and negated or compound conditions... :-)

COBOL is not intended to be written by simpletons using a crayon and 
non-joined-up writing; it is a programming language.

It is reasonable to assume that people using it understand something about 
programming.

I strongly oppose dragging style down to the lowest common denominator so 
that "the guy dragged out of bed at 3 am" doesn't have to scratch his head.

If he was competent he wouldn't be scratching his head. If he is scratching 
his head why did we drag HIM out of bed to make an urgent fix under 
pressure...?  Why employ him at all? Get trained or get out. If you can't 
handle refmodding you might be happier selling burgers...or running for 
Government.

Maybe this is why so many COBOL 'programmers' have difficulty moving to 
other languages where you can't just write what you think in English. 
Programming is a skill and it needs to be learned.

The language is simple and clear enough.

I have never seen a COBOL program yet that I couldn't fix. Even badly 
written ones. Or well written ones that use 'complex' features like those 
described...

I like Frank's PP use and it is only the second time in 40 years that I have 
seen it used in anger.

As for insertion characters, the best solution by far is the one Richard 
mentioned where the Character 'I' can be used to insert anything you like. 
(Great machine, that 1900... :-))

I used to use the following, which doesn't require refmodding or 
INSPECT...(Only because I started using it before refmodding was 
invented...)

01  AUTH-INFO.
    05  AUTH-AMT    PIC ZZZ,ZZ9.99.
    05  PIC X VALUE SPACE.
    05   timeSpot.
            15 tsHours            pic 99.
            15 colon1              pic x.
            15 tsMinutes         pic 99.
            15 colon2              pic x.
            15 tsSeconds        pic 99.
    05  AUTH-TIME   PIC xxBxxBxx redefines timeSpot.

* MY-AMOUNT is 123.45
* TIME-HHMMSS is 124559
MOVE MY-AMOUNT TO AUTH-AMT
MOVE TIME-HHMMSS TO AUTH-TIME
MOVE ':' TO colon1 colon2
DISPLAY  AUTH-INFO

result:
    123.45 12:45:59

But I like Frank's better, assuming you are offended by centiseconds...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T09:42:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net...
>
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't mind PERFORM...VARYING, I don't mind negated or compound conditions, 
and I don't mind reference modification.

The reason I prefer INSPECT to a reference-modified MOVE in this instance is 
that the former is not dependent on the exact placement of the character in 
the PICTURE (or, for that matter, on whether or not there is more than one 
of them in the PICTURE), and the reference modified MOVE is.

Suppose, for example, Management decided they wanted Auth-Time recorded to 
the second instead of merely to the minute as it appears now.  Part of a 
reasonable approach might be to change the declaration to
    05  AUTH-TIME PIC 99B99B99.
This would necessitate NO change to the INSPECT, whereas an additional MOVE 
would be required.   (Actually, I prefer to use "/" in both the PICTURE and 
the INSPECT in such a case, because it's clearer in both locations what's 
being replaced)

> COBOL is not intended to be written by simpletons using a crayon and 
> non-joined-up writing; it is a programming language.

I'm not sure why that makes a MOVE (or two MOVEs) that blindly insert(s) 
characters into certain locations of some data item declared elsewhere a 
neater, better, clearer solution than a single INSPECT that identifies the 
characters being replaced as well as the replacement text!   I think the 
days of "the more obscure and terse my code, the more likely management is 
going to think I'm a genius" have passed us by!   I grant that it's very 
likely a single reference-modified MOVE will be more efficient at run time 
than the INSPECT, but I wouldn't guarantee that being the case with multiple 
MOVEs.

> It is reasonable to assume that people using it understand something about 
> programming.

And ... ?

> I strongly oppose dragging style down to the lowest common denominator so 
> that "the guy dragged out of bed at 3 am" doesn't have to scratch his 
> head.

And ... ?

> If he was competent he wouldn't be scratching his head. If he is 
> scratching his head why did we drag HIM out of bed to make an urgent fix 
> under pressure...?  Why employ him at all? Get trained or get out. If you 
> can't handle refmodding you might be happier selling burgers...or running 
> for Government.

And ... ?

> Maybe this is why so many COBOL 'programmers' have difficulty moving to 
> other languages where you can't just write what you think in English. 
> Programming is a skill and it needs to be learned.

Is this turning into a rant?  All this over the suggestion that the intent 
of a particular INSPECT might be a little easier to figure out *quickly* 
than the intent of a reference-modified MOVE?

> The language is simple and clear enough.
>
> I have never seen a COBOL program yet that I couldn't fix. Even badly 
> written ones. Or well written ones that use 'complex' features like those 
> described...

Complex is in the eye of a beholder.

> I like Frank's PP use and it is only the second time in 40 years that I 
> have seen it used in anger.

I have no objection to the PP use, nor did I raise one.  I was talking about 
the replacement of characters in a numeric-edited data item once its numeric 
value has been established.

My opinion is that it is far more likely that the reference-modified MOVE 
will be the source of a future bug if the format of the destination data 
item is changed than if an INSPECT were used to accomplish the same thing.

> As for insertion characters, the best solution by far is the one Richard 
> mentioned where the Character 'I' can be used to insert anything you like. 
> (Great machine, that 1900... :-))

I agree; that's why I implemented it that way.

> I used to use the following, which doesn't require refmodding or 
> INSPECT...(Only because I started using it before refmodding was 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> DISPLAY  AUTH-INFO

I actually like that better than the reference modification.  I think it's 
clearer code.  In your example you don't have to look back at the 
declaration of AUTH-TIME to have an idea what's intended.  With the 
Reference Modification MOVE, you do.

> But I like Frank's better, assuming you are offended by centiseconds...:-)

Better still, maintain your times in Standard Numeric Time Form and use the 
ISO 8601 time/date functions to format them!   ;-)

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T09:53:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
PS:

Pete wrote:

>> People who say refmodding is difficult are usually the same people who 
>> ban PERFORM...VARYING and negated or compound conditions... :-)

I replied:

> I don't mind PERFORM...VARYING, I don't mind negated or compound 
> conditions, and I don't mind reference modification.

I forgot to reiterate that I *do* mind *abbreviated* combined relation 
conditions.  I believe the reasonable expectation of a native speaker of 
English is that an elision of a comparand or a comparison operator implies 
some sort of closer connection between the elided components and those that 
are explicit, and that is *not* the way it works in COBOL.  I think some 
aspects of this were overlooked when abbreviations were allowed VERY early 
in the development of COBOL; our reference manuals now clarify the "yonder 
be dragons" issues involved here.   What's proper to do in COBOL to produce 
a desired determination of a condition may not be proper to do in English, 
and vice versa.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T17:28:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens<charles.stevens@unisys.com> 05/26/06 10:53 AM >>>
>PS:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>English is that an elision of a comparand or a comparison operator implies

>some sort of closer connection between the elided components and those that

>are explicit, and that is *not* the way it works in COBOL.  I think some 
>aspects of this were overlooked when abbreviations were allowed VERY early

>in the development of COBOL; our reference manuals now clarify the "yonder

>be dragons" issues involved here.   What's proper to do in COBOL to produce

>a desired determination of a condition may not be proper to do in English,

>and vice versa.

I continue to use abbreviated conditions, and I continue to get bit by
them!
It's 40 years to late, most likely, it seems to me (and probably most?) that
the following:

1) if result = 'a' or 'b' and something-is-true
means
2) if (result = 'a' or result = 'b') and something-is-true

when in fact in COBOL it really means
3) if result = 'a' or result = 'b' and something-is-true
which of course means
4) if (result = 'a') or (result = 'b' and something-is-true)

Ah well...  I will most likely continue to use them and be bitten by them,
simply because I like to code as few characters as possible.  The use of
88-levels at least somewhat mitigates the problem, though.  eg
if good-result and something-is-true
where 88 good-result value 'a' 'b'.

That's probably the best.  IMO.  And it makes it fairly simple to add a new
'good result' if needed.

Anyway, no, I don't expect COBOL to change this at this stage.  Would break
too many programs, though I do find it hard to imagine someone using example
1 and really expecting it to bind like example 3.  Has anyone ever done this
intentionally?

Does anyone know of any other programming languages with abbreviated
conditions?  How do they handle them?
(It's ironic that COBOL has them, as COBOL is generally known as being more
verbose than most languages!)

Frank



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Combined Abbreviated contidionts (was: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T23:49:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fuMdg.90924$lk7.26702@fe04.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net>`

```
Frank and Chuck
   (As Roger - and other implementors may want to tell you), I think the 
"oddest" (least desirable?) thing was that when EVALUATE was extended in the '02 
Standard to allow "partial expressions" (in the WHEN clause) that there was no 
decision to PROHIBIT combined abbreviated conditions in this NEW syntax.

I suppose that I understand (and might even have agreed) with NOT having 
different rules for conditional expressions in EVALUATE and IF - on the other 
hand, this would have been a "nice" to  restrict such things as:

  Evaluate Some-Field
      When "A"
       When Not < "B" or C-Field
       When = "D" or E-field or X > "Y"
       When >= "F" or G-Field or NOT  Some-88-level

Oh well,
   so many things could be done differently - if we were ONLY starting over <G>
```

```
Couldn't agree more, Bill.
I sent Bill some questionable syntax off-list some time ago.
I suppose the main question here is :
Are the EVALUATE/WHEN conditional (partial) expressions
directly (one-to-one) equivalent to the corresponding IF syntax.
(And one of the problems is when NOT immediately follows WHEN -
 think about it)

Roger
```

```
> I never thought that typing in a pair of parenthesis was more onerous
> of a task than being bitten.

I put paranthees around all conditions and sub conditions wherever
possible in all languages that I use.  I don't get bitten by
precededence or association problems and I never need to know nor care
what they are in the absence of parentheses, nor what is different
between languages.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<4e42e7F1cn8ffU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net> <fkio72p29bvf593p5ku9ivdsmemh5fiqfc@4ax.com> <1149020416.136520.262110@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4e42e7F1cn8ffU1@individual.net>`

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

No. As you say one set is required and one set is all that is needed
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Roger While" <simrw@sim-basis.de>
- **Date:** 2006-05-31T09:00:59+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e5jevh$i1u$02$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net> <fkio72p29bvf593p5ku9ivdsmemh5fiqfc@4ax.com> <1149020416.136520.262110@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4e42e7F1cn8ffU1@individual.net> <1149033763.569552.14420@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com>`

```
Well, you wouldn't get the first example
into Linux kernel code :-)
Doing an assignment and test in one sentence is
frowned upon and would not be accepted.
In C code it is not just parentheses -
if (count == 1) {
      dosomething();
}
Why the braces ?, Well if you stick something else in,
you don't have to think about the (then necessary) braces.
This is what we do in OpenCOBOL. ( And before
anybody says "but in xxx.c", note that some C code
is generated by the bison/flex parsers over which we
do not have any control.
Note that, in the C code generated by the OC compiler from Cobol,
that we over-parenthesize/bracketize (are there such words?) so
as to be sure that the resultant code is valid.

Roger

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> schrieb im Newsbeitrag 
news:1149033763.569552.14420@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com...
>
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-05-31T00:37:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1149061057.191850.294570@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<e5jevh$i1u$02$1@news.t-online.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net> <fkio72p29bvf593p5ku9ivdsmemh5fiqfc@4ax.com> <1149020416.136520.262110@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4e42e7F1cn8ffU1@individual.net> <1149033763.569552.14420@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <e5jevh$i1u$02$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

Roger While wrote:

> Well, you wouldn't get the first example
> into Linux kernel code :-)
> Doing an assignment and test in one sentence is
> frowned upon and would not be accepted.

I use Kernighan and Ritchie as my guide to style in C   ;-)

      while ( c = fgets(...) )
          {
          ....
          }

As an idiom it is quite useful as it is not necessary to duplicate the
test or the read. Typically this could be:

      c = fgets(..);
      while ( c )
          {
          ...
          c = fgets(...);
          }

as is done in Cobol.  In fact I was arguing against side effects in
conditional expressions just last week.

I tend towards using python where there are iterators:

       fd = open(filename, "r")
       for line in fd:
             dosomething(line)

and this doesn't need {} as it uses indentation to give structure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-31T10:42:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4e5varF1cruckU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net> <fkio72p29bvf593p5ku9ivdsmemh5fiqfc@4ax.com> <1149020416.136520.262110@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4e42e7F1cn8ffU1@individual.net> <1149033763.569552.14420@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <e5jevh$i1u$02$1@news.t-online.com> <1149061057.191850.294570@i40g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 05/31/06 1:37 AM >>>
>
>Roger While wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>          }

It's been a while (20 years???) since I looked at K&R, but does it really
recommend indented braces like that?  I'm used to
while (c = fgets())
{
    dosomethinghere();
}

Though I did see some C code just recently that had the indented braces.

>As an idiom it is quite useful as it is not necessary to duplicate the
>test or the read. Typically this could be:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>and this doesn't need {} as it uses indentation to give structure.

I haven't really used Python, but I'm torn on using indentation to give
structure.  Good things are 1) It encourages (demands!) properly indented
programs, and 2) it saves "space" on the page by not requiring block
terminators.  But it also seems to me it could cause some problems.  That's
a pretty vague fear, as I'm not sure exactly what those problems mght be.

I do love iterators, though!  The above in Ruby would be

fd = File.open(filename, "r")
for line in fd
    dosomething(line)
end

Though that is pretty much just "syntactic sugar" for the more "Rubyesque":

fd = File.open(filename, "r")
fd.each do |line|
    dosomething(line)
end

In fact Ruby even offers the interesting, if not exactly obvious:

File.open(filename, "r") do |fd|
    fd.each do |line|
        dosomething(line)
    end
end

Which opens the file, passes it the block consisting of
do |fd|
    fd.each do |line|
        dosomething(line)
    end
end

and even automatically closes the file when its done.

You could even use curly braces and get:
File.open(filename, "r") { |fd| fd.each { |line| dosomething(line) } }

But I don't find that to be all that readable...

Totally off the current subject, but I find it interesting.

Frank


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

```

Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> It's been a while (20 years???) since I looked at K&R, but does it really
> recommend indented braces like that?  I'm used to

No, but I don't like any other style.

> I haven't really used Python, but I'm torn on using indentation to give
> structure.  Good things are 1) It encourages (demands!) properly indented
> programs, and 2) it saves "space" on the page by not requiring block
> terminators.  But it also seems to me it could cause some problems.  That's
> a pretty vague fear, as I'm not sure exactly what those problems mght be.

Those aren't the actual advantage, it is that the viewer and the
compiler 'see' exactly the same program structure. And so will the next
programmer.

Of course you have to ensure that the editor doesn't change tabs to
spaces or vv. I use editor settings that shows tabs if there are ever
there and never outputs them.

> I do love iterators, though!  The above in Ruby would be
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> But I don't find that to be all that readable...

One of the _advantages_ of python is that mostly there is _just_one_
way of doing things.  One of the failures of PERL (for me) is that it
can be written so many different ways. This means that I can pick up a
PERL program and it may look like well written C, or it may be chicken
scratchings. With Python it always looks like Python.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-31T10:24:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4e5u9gF1d1ndgU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e57bpi$17g5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dph9lF1apmv4U1@individual.net> <fkio72p29bvf593p5ku9ivdsmemh5fiqfc@4ax.com> <1149020416.136520.262110@i39g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4e42e7F1cn8ffU1@individual.net> <1149033763.569552.14420@u72g2000cwu.googlegroups.com> <e5jevh$i1u$02$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
That comment about Linux kernel code is interesting.  I always thought that
one of the advantages to C (and other languages that offer this) was that
you *can* do assignment and test in one statement.  Are you saying that the
following would not be acceptable for Linux kernel code?

while ((a = readrec()) != -1) {
    dosomethinghere();
}

One of my huge pet peeves about COBOL is that you must do a "priming read",
ie:

PERFORM READ-A-RECORD
PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
    PERFORM PROCESS-A-RECORD
    PERFORM READ-A-RECORD
END-PERFORM

I would much prefer something like

read-a-record
perform until read-a-record = end-of-file
    process-a-record
end-perform

(yeah, that's a little strange looking, but I hope my point is clear)

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

>>> Roger While<simrw@sim-basis.de> 05/31/06 1:00 AM >>>
Well, you wouldn't get the first example
into Linux kernel code :-)
Doing an assignment and test in one sentence is
frowned upon and would not be accepted.
In C code it is not just parentheses -
if (count == 1) {
      dosomething();
}
Why the braces ?, Well if you stick something else in,
you don't have to think about the (then necessary) braces.
This is what we do in OpenCOBOL. ( And before
anybody says "but in xxx.c", note that some C code
is generated by the bison/flex parsers over which we
do not have any control.
Note that, in the C code generated by the OC compiler from Cobol,
that we over-parenthesize/bracketize (are there such words?) so
as to be sure that the resultant code is valid.

Roger
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-05-27T13:18:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dpnmjF1acmgrU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No big deal.

> Suppose, for example, Management decided they wanted Auth-Time recorded to 
> the second instead of merely to the minute as it appears now.

Bloody Management... they're the bane of our lives... :-)

> Part of a reasonable approach might be to change the declaration to
>    05  AUTH-TIME PIC 99B99B99.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> locations what's being replaced)

The only reason you need to know what's being replaced is because you are 
replacing it! If you didn't use INSPECT in this instance, it would be 
totally irrelevant.

Two refmodded MOVES (resolved at compile time) are STILL many times more 
efficient than your INSPECT. You had to change the code to extend the 
picture; adding the extra refmodded operand to the existing MOVE is part of 
the same operation.

>
>> COBOL is not intended to be written by simpletons using a crayon and 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> characters being replaced as well as the replacement text!

First off, the characters are not 'blindly inserted' they are carefully 
inserted at the precise locations where they need to be, and this is 
resolved at compile time.

Second off, there is absolutely no need whatsoever to identify the 
'characters to be replaced' (as I pointed out above.)

Thirdly, the less code you write, the less chance there is of error.

Fourthly, the simpler the constructs you use, the less chance there is of 
error. MOVE is much simpler than INSPECT, even leaving aside the obvious 
execution efficiency. I support using 'complex constructs' but NOT where a 
simple one will do, and NOT where the clarity of the code is compromised. 
(MOVE is very uncompromising... :-))

> I think the days of "the more obscure and terse my code, the more likely 
> management is going to think I'm a genius" have passed us by!

I wouldn't know, as that has never been a consideration in my coding.  I 
learned many years ago that trying to impress Management is a thankless 
task; since then, I have concentrated on trying to impress me (it's much 
more difficult; but, on the reare occasions when I manage it, it feels real 
good :-))

>  I grant that it's very likely a single reference-modified MOVE will be 
> more efficient at run time than the INSPECT, but I wouldn't guarantee that 
> being the case with multiple MOVEs.

What, all two of them? :-)  Thank you for (reluctantly) supporting my case. 
I have to smile at 'very likely'  and 'guarantee' in the above... when does 
'very likely' become 'certainty' in your life, Chuck? :-)

Here is an unequivocal statement:
<unequivocal statement>
"Two fixed reference modified MOVEs, inserting a single character into a 
string will ALWAYS result in better object efficiency than an INSPECT that 
does the same job, ON ANY PLATFORM!"
</unequivocal statement>

Not only that, but the source is clearer too, and it requires typing less 
characters. <may not be the case...arguable based on perception and opinion>

(See, I can tolerate certainty in my life...once the wave collapses I can 
live with it...:-))

>
>> It is reasonable to assume that people using it understand something 
>> about programming.
>
> And ... ?

read on, there's more... :-)

>
>> I strongly oppose dragging style down to the lowest common denominator so 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> And ... ?

read on, there's more... :-)
>
>> If he was competent he wouldn't be scratching his head. If he is 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> And ... ?

read on,. there's more... :-)

>> Maybe this is why so many COBOL 'programmers' have difficulty moving to 
>> other languages where you can't just write what you think in English. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> than the intent of a reference-modified MOVE?

Well, I haven't had a rant for a while so I'm entitled. :-) I plough through 
the tedious stuff you post on WG4 and J4 so indulge me...

The above was not prompted by "the suggestion that the intent of a 
particular INSPECT might be a little easier to figure out *quickly*  than 
the intent of a reference-modified MOVE?", although that was a part of it.

The case I took some time to present above was prompted much more by the 
syndrome you demonstrated: "Let's use something that will be clearer (when 
it isn't) because we must really consider the maintenance programmer..."

We see it here all the time. "Ease of maintenance" being used to justify bad 
constructs, verbosity,  and inefficient code when, as has been pointed out 
here many times, people consider 'what they are used to' to be 'easily 
maintainable'. I have no idea who will be maintaining my code at 3 am but 
I'll work on the assumption  whoever it is will be a competent COBOL 
programmer, and it could be me in two years time. So I'll document what I 
did and keep it as clear as possible, biut not to the point of using an 
INSPECT where a MOVE would do...

>
>> The language is simple and clear enough.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Complex is in the eye of a beholder.

Precisely.
>
>> I like Frank's PP use and it is only the second time in 40 years that I 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> numeric value has been established.

And I was pointing out that such replacement is unnecessary and pointless.

>
> My opinion is that it is far more likely that the reference-modified MOVE 
> will be the source of a future bug if the format of the destination data 
> item is changed than if an INSPECT were used to accomplish the same thing.
>

Well, that is your opinion. My opinion about your opinion...? Bollocks!!

So, like the civilized gentlemen we are, we can agree to differ... :-)

(It's not like it was IMPORTANT or anything... we're talking about a dying 
language here...)

>> As for insertion characters, the best solution by far is the one Richard 
>> mentioned where the Character 'I' can be used to insert anything you 
>> like. (Great machine, that 1900... :-))
>
> I agree; that's why I implemented it that way.

Good thing we don't always disagree... :-)

>
>> I used to use the following, which doesn't require refmodding or 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> clearer code.

I'm not sure it is clearer, but it served me well for several decades... :-)


 In your example you don't have to look back at the
> declaration of AUTH-TIME to have an idea what's intended.  With the 
> Reference Modification MOVE, you do.
>
Or not... :-) It is a date/time; most people have a fair idea of how these 
are usually formatted...

>> But I like Frank's better, assuming you are offended by 
>> centiseconds...:-)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So many options; so short a life... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T22:20:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zHOdg.2389$ho5.189403@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4dpnmjF1acmgrU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dpnmjF1acmgrU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
> news:e57b55$174i$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
…[280 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
In My opinion, they are both obscure. I'd redefine it, give it a name as
Grace intended, and do something like:

	MOVE ":" TO IMBEDDED-COLIN-POSITION.

Then it is defined in the data structure, and readable in the procedure.

I rather like reference modification, but reserve it for times when I
need to use a variable. Using it to avoid having to define the structure
is laziness, or perhaps better, a shortcut.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-05-27T02:55:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e58f2f$6b5$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>I think it is appalling. Why would I use an INSPECT when I can use a MOVE?

I barely know why *I* would do things, Mr Dashwood, let alone anyone 
else.

>
>People who say refmodding is difficult are usually the same people who ban 
>PERFORM...VARYING and negated or compound conditions... :-)

I've never run across anyone who said reference modification is 
difficult, Mr Dashwood, but I, personally, have, at times, found it 
necessary to put more effort into understanding what is intended by 
A17538-CUST-MAST(4381:2) than A17538-CUST-MAST-STATE... but that's just 
me, don'cha know.

>
>COBOL is not intended to be written by simpletons using a crayon and 
>non-joined-up writing; it is a programming language.

What something is intended for, Mr Dashwood, and what it gets used for 
might just, possibly, not be the same things; I have seen folks use a 
torque-wrench as a hammer.

>
>It is reasonable to assume that people using it understand something about 
>programming.

It is reasonable to assume that a corporation will try to put as little 
money as possible into fulfilling a corporate requirement... and that 
includes staffing, Mr Dashwood.

>
>I strongly oppose dragging style down to the lowest common denominator so 
>that "the guy dragged out of bed at 3 am" doesn't have to scratch his head.

I strongly oppose anything which impedes the completion of the 
nightly/weekly/quarterly/annual batch run within its allotted window, Mr 
Dashwood... and I do that not only as a matter of professional pride but 
because that is what my clients pay me to do.  Head-scratching takes 
time and time is money.

(Interestingly enough... space is time, too, so space is money... 
perhaps that is why folks pay rent.)

[snip]

>The language is simple and clear enough.

It is my experience, Mr Dashwood, that clarity is in the mind of the 
beholder.

>
>I have never seen a COBOL program yet that I couldn't fix.

As my Sainted Paternal Grandfather - may he sleep with the angels! - 
used to day, 'Never use yourself as a comparative... you'll only get 
disappointed.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2006-05-28T02:09:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dr4tfF1brae4U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com> <e52fu5$1cm9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e58f2f$6b5$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e58f2f$6b5$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Presumably you know why you posted this response? :-)

>>
>>People who say refmodding is difficult are usually the same people who ban
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> me, don'cha know.

You made that up.

>
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If it moves the object being struck, I don't see that as bad. Particularly 
in the absence of a hammer... Anyway, it has no relevance to the intended 
purpose fo COBOL, which is what I was discussing.

>>
>>It is reasonable to assume that people using it understand something about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> includes staffing, Mr Dashwood.

That would be self defeating and is therefore NOT a reasonable assumption. 
Many corporations are happy to put plenty of money into fulfilling corporate 
requirements. Enlightened companies know they need to do this.

I've just spent a considerable amount of time and effort getting training 
for people on my team, which will involve a serious amount of the company's 
money, so your sweeping statement is as incorrect as your tediously biased 
observations on corporate cultures.

None of the people who I needed to get approval from for this expenditure 
hesitated to give it. In fact, it was done gladly, as the training budget 
had not been fully used, and the effect of training on morale and efficiency 
is well recognised by the company.

Similarly with staffing. People I have interviewed and recommended have been 
taken on board at salaries which reflect their value. Again, there is no 
problem with this at senior management level. It is seen as investing in the 
future of the company.

And this is NOT the only company where I have seen such a culture.  Yes, 
there are bad corporate cultures (and you seem to delight in working for 
them then complaining about it here), but there are also good places to work 
and there are just as many good managers as there are bad ones... (Maybe not 
in New York; my experience there is limited.).

Just because you live in a dark world doesn't mean that we all do.

>
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dashwood...

So you probably wouldn't use INSPECT where a MOVE would do... :-)

> and I do that not only as a matter of professional pride but
> because that is what my clients pay me to do.  Head-scratching takes
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

But you hardly know why you think that, so your thoughts on it may not be 
clear, right... ;-)

>>
>>I have never seen a COBOL program yet that I couldn't fix.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And, as I have responded before to this well-worn chestnut, I am yet to be 
disappointed by such a comparison.

My point was that COBOL is an easily maintained language, even when it is 
written badly (but you snipped the context so as not to miss a chance at 
trotting out the same old hackneyed Granddad cliche.) When was the last time 
you had an original thought?

Maybe Granddad was wrong?

If you think my tone is sharper than usal, it is. It isn't personal, I'm 
just generally pissed off with the negativity here.

(Actually, having said that, I feel much better now... remembered it is only 
CLC :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2006-05-29T09:40:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e5efij$aaf$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <4dojm7F1bt62kU1@individual.net> <e58f2f$6b5$1@reader1.panix.com> <4dr4tfF1brae4U1@individual.net>`

```
In article <4dr4tfF1brae4U1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:e58f2f$6b5$1@reader1.panix.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>Presumably you know why you posted this response? :-)

Perhaps so, perhaps no... perhaps... barely.

>
>>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>You made that up.

I have seen the likes of that and worse in Prod-implemented code, Mr 
Dashwood; if my memory - which is, I admit, porous - transposed A17583 to 
A17538 then so be it.  Whether recalled with jot-and-tittle perfection or 
otherwise the statement might appear to have equal validity.

>>>COBOL is not intended to be written by simpletons using a crayon and
>>>non-joined-up writing; it is a programming language.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>purpose fo COBOL, which is what I was discussing.

My apologies for my obscurity, Mr Dashwood; what I had hoped to convey is 
that if one chooses to use a torque-wrench as a hammer - as I have seen 
folks do - then one might, possibly, forfeit the possibility that others, 
upon hearing the hammer-wrench folks complain that the torque-wrench was 
damaged during this use, would have much sympathy.

In the case of a programming language it is true that the language will, 
most likely, not sustain damage to the point of being unuseable (as the 
torque-wrench did)... but one might do well to keep in mind the concept of 
'use the right tool for the right job'.

>
>>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>That would be self defeating and is therefore NOT a reasonable assumption. 

It is what I have seen happen, Mr Dashwood, and therefore - reasonable or 
not - it is something for which I try to prepare myself.

>Many corporations are happy to put plenty of money into fulfilling corporate 
>requirements. Enlightened companies know they need to do this.

I barely know what *I* know, Mr Dashwood... and I seem to recall more than 
one or two folks posting to this group whose comments might cause a 
conclusion that the 'enlightened companies' which you posit are few and 
far between.

>
>I've just spent a considerable amount of time and effort getting training 
>for people on my team, which will involve a serious amount of the company's 
>money, so your sweeping statement is as incorrect as your tediously biased 
>observations on corporate cultures.

Hmmmmm... how many Hirundidinidae do a summer make, Mr Dashwood?  I speak 
from my experience, you speak from yours... and only one of us, to the 
best of my recollection, refers to the other as 'incorrect' or 'tedious'.  
How you handle your group just might, possibly, be what makes you 
different in your position and more effective in your accomplishments.

>
>None of the people who I needed to get approval from for this expenditure 
>hesitated to give it. In fact, it was done gladly, as the training budget 
>had not been fully used, and the effect of training on morale and efficiency 
>is well recognised by the company.

I'm just drippingly *wet* with happiness for you, Mr Dashwood... and I 
also recall what my Sainted Paternal Grandfather said about using yourself 
as a comparative.

[snip]

>Just because you live in a dark world doesn't mean that we all do.

Mr Dashwood, I post about what I have lived through and seen, that you 
choose to see it as 'dark' appears to differ from my conclusion of it as 
merely... that which I have seen.

>>>I strongly oppose dragging style down to the lowest common denominator so
>>>that "the guy dragged out of bed at 3 am" doesn't have to scratch his 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>So you probably wouldn't use INSPECT where a MOVE would do... :-)

I'll burn that bridge when I come to it, Mr Dashwood.

>
>> and I do that not only as a matter of professional pride but
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>clear, right... ;-)

That might depend on the mind beholding them, Mr Dashwood... and to the 
best of my understanding my thoughts (barring those pesky 
brainwave-stealing Space Aliens, of course) are never beheld, merely my 
attempts to transmit them to others.

>
>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>disappointed by such a comparison.

At one point, Mr Dashwood, I was never disappointed in the knowledge that 
the next day I would wake up pale, frail, beardless and soprano... and 
then enough time passed for me to experience what happened when puberty 
hit.

>
>My point was that COBOL is an easily maintained language, even when it is 
>written badly (but you snipped the context so as not to miss a chance at 
>trotting out the same old hackneyed Granddad cliche.) When was the last time 
>you had an original thought?

That might depend on whom you ask, Mr Dashwood... according to something 
written a few millennia back there is nothing new under the sun.

>
>Maybe Granddad was wrong?

Could well be... but he appears to have his usual accuracy here.

>
>If you think my tone is sharper than usal, it is. It isn't personal, I'm 
>just generally pissed off with the negativity here.

If it isn't personal, Mr Dashwood, then I wonder how the labelling another 
person as 'incorrect', 'tedious', 'living in a dark world' and questioning 
as to whether they ever had 'an original thought' addresses anything other 
than the hominem.

>
>(Actually, having said that, I feel much better now... remembered it is only 
>CLC :-))

Oh, I *cannot* resist...

... not to worry, Mr Dashwood... some people have seen that others, 
convinced of their own righteousness, have a difficulty keeping more than 
one simple thought in view at times.  In this case that simple thought 
might well be 'To say 'I have seen thus' is not equal to saying 'Thus it 
is, was and will be in all places at all times.''

DD
```

```
:-)

While I haven't made it my life's work, I confess to being amused at the 
antics of rabbits in the late afternoon on a Summer day...

There is a local landmark (Mt. Maunganui) near where I live and sometimes, 
if you just sit down quietly on the slopes of it and observe the container 
ships and pleasure craft entering the Port, after about 10 minutes you will 
become aware that there are rabbits all around you. As long as you don't 
move to stand up, they will carry on as if you weren't there, exploring, 
feeding and humping as rabbits are wont to do. I have had some of them 
approach really close (probably local people feed them from picnic 
leftovers).

When you first look at a bunch of rabbits they all look more or less the 
same, but if you observe carefully, you can begin to recognise individuals. 
The next step is to figure out which ones are probably related (to each 
other...:-))..."Oooh look, there's Uncle Roger and cousin Roberta...". 
This is a subject that definitely deserves far more study and the US 
Government might do well not to ignore it..

For all we know, the rabbits could be organising...

That famous Irish radical rabbit, Paisley O'Hare, could even now be planning 
a network of burroughs (Oops, sorry...Unisys)  to attack the free world from 
below.  Maybe they want proper and equitable distribution of carrots for 
everyone; they might be working on a vitamin A bomb; or Osama Bin Bunny 
might be planning to hop into Guantanamo Bay and organise a "Lettuce Outa 
Here!" protest...

Without information, we are the bunnies...

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P put to real use

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-24T17:38:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dk93vF1apjq9U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4djq2cF1a59r0U1@individual.net> <gje972t5q5fp4a7oltmj5qh095vg8bvpe0@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee<howard@brazee.net> 05/24/06 1:57 PM >>>
>On Wed, 24 May 2006 13:21:47 -0600, "Frank Swarbrick"
><Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers scratch their heads.

TIME-HHMMSS is a PIC 9(7) COMP-3.  I don't think ref-mod would be much help
here without using a temp field and some other things.  And I wouldn't even
be using ref-mod if I could declare AUTH-TIME as PIC 99:99.  For example, by
date one looks like this:

01  AUTH-INFO.
    05  AUTH-AMT    PIC ZZZ,ZZ9.99.
    05  PIC X VALUE SPACE.
    05  AUTH-DATE  PIC 99/99PP.
    05  AUTH-TIME   PIC 99B99PP.

MOVE DATE-MMDDYY TO AUTH-DATE

Which gives me:
09/25
(Sept 25)

Frank



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ridin' tall on the Santiago Canyon Road (was Re: What could J4 (or WG4) do)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-25T13:00:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e552dn$2tk7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<e54hi5$2jks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <sr2dnfVYINJ4muvZnZ2dneKdnZydnZ2d@adelphia.com>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@adelphia.net> wrote in message 
news:sr2dnfVYINJ4muvZnZ2dneKdnZydnZ2d@adelphia.com...
> Chuck,
> You got me to read a ways into your anecdote, because you wrote "bike" and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mounts, I wish the term would only be applied to bicycles.

Well, they both have two wheels, thus "bi-cycle".   Moreover, since the 
modern motorcycle arguably has its origins in the motorized bicycle, I'm not 
sure the exclusivity claim can be successfully made!   ;-)

BMW riders insist, with similar success, that BEEMERs always have TWO wheels 
and that it's BIMMERs that have four.  We can insist all we want ...  ;-)

I also would suggest that some of my more grizzled friends would be amused 
by the premise that only those riding unmotorized two-wheelers had the right 
to call themselves, or be called,  "bikers" ...    ;-)

For a long time I referred to myself as a "motorcyclist" rather than as a 
"biker".  After my solo run to Texas devoid of windscreen, complete with a 
1200-mile run in 21 hours on the return trip, I think I earned the latter 
title, thank you very much ...

A friend of mine who races unmotorized two-wheelers but has sought out the 
friendship of a group of motorcyclists is referred to by them as being an 
"acoustic biker" ...

       -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-3d25e4ed09"></a>
## Thread: Accept / Display - an "environment variables — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Accept / Display - an "environment variables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T17:17:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%KGdg.14295$sY5.9484@fe10.news.easynews.com>`

```
(Follow-on to other thread),

Currently, ANSI/ISO Standards allow for ACCEPT FROM DAY/DATE/DAY-OF-WEEK/TIME 
(and "enhanced screen stuff")

All "other" ACCEPTS need to be to a mnemonic associated with "hardware-ish" 
device via the Special-Names paragraph.

The reason that I mention this, is that IBM, Micro Focus, and possibly others 
have an extension that allow one to directly reference such "hardware-ish" 
device in the Procedure Division - NOT via the mnemonic-name, e.g.

    Accept Some-Field from Sysout.

(This is marked as a "compatibility" feature with IBM's '74 Standard compiler - 
I don't know if it was an extension or part of that Standard.  I know it is NOT 
allowed in the '85 Standard.)

I (personally) find it unlikely that J4/WG4 would add syntax/keyword such as

     Accept whatever from ENVIRONMENT-NAME

There is certainly no guarantee that they would or would not reject this, but my 
best guess is that *IF* the ACCEPT/DISPLAY approach were used, that it WOULD 
still be via a "mnemonic-name" associated in the Special-Names paragraph.
```

#### ↳ Re: Accept / Display - an "environment variables

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T10:44:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e57ep8$1983$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<%KGdg.14295$sY5.9484@fe10.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:%KGdg.14295$sY5.9484@fe10.news.easynews.com...

> The reason that I mention this, is that IBM, Micro Focus, and possibly 
> others have an extension that allow one to directly reference such 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I know it is NOT allowed in the '85 Standard.)

Anything but a mnemonic-name defined in special-names in a Format 1 ACCEPT 
is definitely an extension to the '74 standard, and from what I can see it's 
an extension to the '68 standard as well (at least, IBM's reference manual 
for DOS COBOL seems to think so).
> I (personally) find it unlikely that J4/WG4 would add syntax/keyword such 
> as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph.

That'd be the way I'd suggest going.  I'm not convinced anything needs to be 
*added* to the language for ACCEPT/DISPLAY; the remaining piece is the 
functionality of SET, as I see it, presuming the semantics of DISPLAY don't 
work for that case.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

---

<a id="t-08006ff839"></a>
## Thread: Accept / Display - an "environment variables — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Accept / Display - an "environment variables

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T12:17:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e57k7r$1cb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:...
>
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>    Accept Some-Field from Sysout.

I'm no IBM expert, but getting input from what sure looks like something 
that belongs in the output-only class seems decidedly counterintuitive to me 
...

Kind of reminds me of something like
    Select ... assign to paper-tape-punch
        organization is indexed record key ...

Ummm... I for one would have some difficulty visualizing that ...

Could you perhaps have meant SysIN?    ;=)

Our default, by the way, is the equivalent of the IBM DOS "ACCEPT ... FROM 
CONSOLE".

Our COBOL74 allows "ACCEPT ... FROM SPO" (would you believe SPO is a 
mnemonic for "Supervisory Print Out" from B5000 days?) and "ACCEPT FROM ODT" 
(where ODT is "operator display terminal" when we finally got away from 
KSR33's), and it allows mnemonics "equated" to those terms in SPECIAL-NAMES.

Our COBOL85 is stricter; it requires a mnemonic for ODT or date/time 
specifier after FROM and doesn't allow SPO as a mnemonic.

Neither one allows the (implicit, in the case of COBOL74, or explicit for 
either one) mnemonic to refer to anything but the ODT.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Accept / Display - an "environment variables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T19:37:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UNIdg.88857$lk7.46067@fe04.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<e57k7r$1cb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
I wondered who would notice that.  My mistake.  "Common" IBM usage is:

 Accept whatever from Sysin
    or
Display whatever upon Sysout

(There are a long list of "valid" things that can be used for Accept/Display - 
e.g. CONSOLE, SYSIPT, etc)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Accept / Display - an "environment variables

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T17:53:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dpinuF1arrshU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<e57k7r$1cb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <UNIdg.88857$lk7.46067@fe04.news.easynews.com>`

```
COBOL for VSE

accept data-area from console
display data-area upon console
accept data-area from sysipt
accept data-area from sysin
display data-area upon syslst
display data-area upon syslist
display data-area upon sysout
display data-area upon syspch
display data-area upon syspunch


And somewhat related, for WRITE ADVANCING:
special-names.
    C01 IS C01
    CSP IS CSP
    AFP-5A IS AFP-5A.
write fd-rec after advancing C01   *> skip to printer "channel 1"
write fd-rec after advancing CSP  *> suppress spacing
and my personal favorite
write fd-rec after advancing AFP-5A  >* special controls for Advanced
Function Printing(tm)

I don't really understand the point of special-names here.  We do generally
have it more like:
    C01 IS CHANNEL-1
    CSP IS SUPRESS-SPACE
    AFP-5A IS AFP-CONTROL.
write fd-rec after advancing CHANNEL-1
write fd-rec after advancing SUPPRESS-SPACE
and my personal favorite
write fd-rec after advancing AFP-CONTROL

So I suppose the requirement to use special-names does make things a bit
more readible, but I don't really see why one cannot use the names
"directly", without utilizing special-names.
Ah well...

One thing I've never done is
special-names.
    console IS cnsl.

display 'something' upon cnsl

It works, but it never occured to me I might want to do that, when
display 'something' upon console
seems (to me) to be quite obvious...

I suppose the special-names paragraph would be useful if I were converting
from COBOL/VSE to some other COBOL.  Then I could do something like...

special-names.
    sysout is syslst
    sysout is syspch
    sysin is sysipt.

(Actually, that does not work because I've assigned two mnemonic names to
the same environment name.  Huh.  Seems like that should work...)

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

>>> William M. Klein<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> 05/26/06 1:37 PM >>>
I wondered who would notice that.  My mistake.  "Common" IBM usage is:

 Accept whatever from Sysin
    or
Display whatever upon Sysout

(There are a long list of "valid" things that can be used for Accept/Display
- 
e.g. CONSOLE, SYSIPT, etc)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accept / Display - an "environment variables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-27T03:54:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s3Qdg.73757$aA3.57135@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<e57k7r$1cb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <UNIdg.88857$lk7.46067@fe04.news.easynews.com> <4dpinuF1arrshU1@individual.net>`

```
The "rationale" for requiring the Special-Names and mnemonic is that it means 
that "device-names" (e.g. SYSOUT, SYSIPT, CONSOLE, etc) need NOT be restricted 
from use as "user-defined words".  However, for implementations (such as IBM's) 
they prohibit these words from being used as user-defined names.
```

```
"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message 
news:4dpinuF1arrshU1@individual.net...
> I don't really understand the point of special-names here.  We do 
> generally

> have it more like:
>    C01 IS CHANNEL-1
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ah well...

For the WRITE ... ADVANCING <mnemonic-name> case, whatever feature-names the 
implementor chooses to use in the particular environment are defined by the 
implementor.  I haven't a clue what AFP-5A means in the Unisys MCP 
environment.  The implementor also defines switch-names and device-names. 
What is SYSIN in one environment may be READER in another, and I'm not 
convinced the standard should support one over another.  Should all 
conforming implementations of COBOL be required to provide syntax for 
testing and setting the bits in the UPSI byte -- and be limited to no more 
than eight switches -- whether or not there is any direct or indirect analog 
to the UPSI byte (by that name or any other) in the implementation?  Same 
sort of thing applies to the likes of "ADVANCING C01".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

## Thread: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements — 9 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T15:37:57-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
All references are to WD 1.6.

Page 73, 8.3.1.1.2.4 Device-name,
"A device-name identifies a hardware or software
device in the operating environment."

Page 215, 12.2.6.1 SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph, GF,
"device-name-1 IS mnemonic-name-3".

Page 217, 12.2.6.2 SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph, SR 7,
"Mnemonic-name-3 may be specified only in the ACCEPT
and DISPLAY statements. The implementor may specify
additional restrictions on the use of mnemonic-names
that reference specific device-names."

Page 437, 14.8.1.1 ACCEPT statement, GF,
"Format 1 (device):"

Page 438, 14.8.1.2 ACCEPT statement, SR 2,
"Mnemonic-name-1 shall be specified in the
SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph of the environment
division and shall be associated with a hardware
or software device in the operating environment."

Page 464, 14.8.10 DISPLAY statement, first paragraph,
"The line format of the DISPLAY statement causes data
to be transferred to a hardware or software device in the
operating environment."

Page 464, 14.8.10.1 DISPLAY statement, GF,
"Format 1 (line):"

Page 464, 14.8.10.2 DISPLAY statement, SR 2,
"Mnemonic-name-1 shall be associated with a device
in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph in the environment
division."

I suggest changing the DISPLAY statement, Format 1,
from "line" to "device" to reflect the fact that rules are
for a "device" and not a "line", which word is only applicable
to those devices that are capable of positioning, and making
the same change to the second word of the first paragraph
so that it reads ""The device format of the DISPLAY
statement causes data to be transferred to a hardware
or software device in the operating environment."

Furthermore, I suggest providing the same wording for
both ACCEPT statement SR2 and DISPLAY statement
SR2, since ultimately the same requirement exists for
mnemonic-name-1 in both statements and suggest that
this change be accomplished by changing ACCEPT
statement SR2 to use the same words as DISPLAY
statement SR2, since the use of "device" resolves to
"device-name", which 8.3.1.1.2.4 states "identifies
a hardware or software device in the operating
environment". (This eliminates the redundancy between
ACCEPT statement SR2 and Device-name, which
redundancy is not present in DISPLAY statement SR2.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T19:49:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gZIdg.65181$Gj2.24692@fe05.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Chuck,
   04-0191- Revisions to "hardware" reference proposals

at:
  http://www.cobolstandard.info/j4/files/04-0191.doc

was your paper.  Can/will you follow up with getting this "considered" by J4 (or 
can you tell us why it shouldn't be)?

(The 03- papers are no longer on line, so I don't know if this was already 
discussed and rejected there)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T14:45:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e57st4$1hgr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com> <gZIdg.65181$Gj2.24692@fe05.news.easynews.com>`

```
The underlying intent of my paper was to eliminate the explicit standard 
requirement that whatever you ACCEPTed from (or DISPLAYed upon) had to be a 
*HARDWARE* device, which is the way it was worded before.

My guess is that these suggestions will be handled editorially, but I will 
certainly look into the issues (and the text history).

    -Chuck Stevens


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:gZIdg.65181$Gj2.24692@fe05.news.easynews.com...
> Chuck,
>   04-0191- Revisions to "hardware" reference proposals
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T14:49:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e57t4q$1hh1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
The intent of *my* change was to eliminate the standards requirement that 
the operating environment had to provide a *hardware device* of some sort 
that ACCEPT and DISPLAY had to deal with directly.  With that requirement in 
place, it would not be possible, say, for a *software* implementation of a 
platform on commodity hardware to provide a COBOL implementation compliant 
with the standard, because what the generated code would be interacting with 
was *software*, not *hardware*.

There may have been an underlying issue that got carried forward from the 
draft at the time, or I might have transcribed something wrong.   I'll 
certainly check into it.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message 
news:127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com...
> All references are to WD 1.6.
>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T18:58:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<127f21gb6kk356f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com> <e57t4q$1hh1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:e57t4q$1hh1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> The intent of *my* change was to eliminate the standards requirement that
> the operating environment had to provide a *hardware device* of some sort
> that ACCEPT and DISPLAY had to deal with directly.  With that requirement
in
> place, it would not be possible, say, for a *software* implementation of a
> platform on commodity hardware to provide a COBOL implementation compliant
> with the standard, because what the generated code would be interacting
with
> was *software*, not *hardware*.
>
> There may have been an underlying issue that got carried forward from the
> draft at the time, or I might have transcribed something wrong.   I'll
> certainly check into it.

ISO 1989:2000, 14.8.10.1 DISPLAY statement, General format,
"Format 1 (line):" may have escaped scrutiny simply because it
did not say "hardware". What I realized was that in WD 1.6 a
Format 1 display will always be to a device whether that device
can position to the next line, or not. Thus, it seems that "device"
will always apply; but "line" not always.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "Roger While" <simrw@sim-basis.de>
- **Date:** 2006-05-27T07:15:46+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e58na5$4g5$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Well, my 2 cents worth.
The problem, in my opinion, is the use of the word "device".
On Posix systems, you never, ever communicate with a hardware device
and, more likely than not, never with a software device (literally taken).
What you communicate with is a resource.

Roger

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-27T19:40:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<127houm6op1imb8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com> <e57t4q$1hh1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <127f21gb6kk356f@corp.supernews.com> <e58na5$4g5$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

"Roger While" <simrw@sim-basis.de> wrote in message
news:e58na5$4g5$03$1@news.t-online.com...
> Well, my 2 cents worth.
> The problem, in my opinion, is the use of the word "device".
> On Posix systems, you never, ever communicate with a hardware device
> and, more likely than not, never with a software device (literally taken).
> What you communicate with is a resource.

The current and draft COBOL standards recognize that
such "devices" are resources.

WD 1.6, Page 210, 12.2 Configuration section,
"The configuration section specifies aspects of the data
processing system that are dependent on the specific
system as well as special control techniques and a means
of associating a local name with an external resource."
```

#### ↳ Re: Minor? editorial correction for ACCEPT and DISPLAY statements

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-31T12:48:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e5kruq$3t4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<127emajf583ddd2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I've submitted this suggestion for posting as J4 Document 06-0051 on the J4 
website for consideration by the committee in their July/August meeting, and 
am working on a document with the details of the corrections, mostly as you 
suggest.

The one thing I disagree with you on is the replacement of the wording in 
ACCEPT Syntax Rule 2 with that found in DISPLAY Syntax Rule 2; I personally 
prefer the longer, more explicit (and, yes, redundant) wording in ACCEPT be 
used in both places because the *previous* standard explicitly required that 
the mnemonic name "be associated with a hardware device" in both rules.  The 
draft *no longer* requires that the device be a *hardware* device; that's 
the point.

If this weren't something that changed, yes, I think we could allow the 
chain of references to take care of it, but when the basic idea changes in a 
rule, I think it's appropriate to highlight the change, and this changed 
(for both ACCEPT and DISPLAY).

My recommendation to J4 will be that DISPLAY SR2 be changed to read like 
ACCEPT SR2 rather than vice versa as you suggest.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

## Thread: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** hcmason@sbcglobal.net
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T15:09:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello:
Someone has given me some variable length records to process.  No
copylib record layouts are available.  Is there any way to tell how big
the record is that was just read?

ISPF says the input file has record length of 2075 (includes the 4
bytes size of record field)
Block size = 27998, recfm=VB.

My code in the data division:
001900  FD  TEXT-VAR-FILE
 002000      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
 002100 * I THINK THE REAL LRECL IS 2075, BUT 4 BYTES USED FOR
 002200 * RECORD LENGTH
 002300      RECORD CONTAINS 80 TO 2071 CHARACTERS.
 002400  01  TEXT-VAR-REC-80                PIC X(80).
 002500  01  TEXT-VAR-RECORD                PIC X(2071).

Actually, I'm not sure about the smallest record...

Is there a special register in cobol, or anythig to tell what the
length really was???

006200  Z0000-READ-INPUT.
 006210 D    DISPLAY 'Z0000-READ-INPUT'
 006300      READ TEXT-VAR-FILE
 006400         AT END
 006500             MOVE 'Y'      TO WS-EOF-SWITCH
 006600      END-READ.

I'm planning on moving the entire record to a character array to pull
out the eye readable text...Unfortunately I'm not sure how long each
record will be, so I many end up getting bad results as I try to find
the last eye readable word, or terminate the parsing after the last
word/end of record....

Chris.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T22:23:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XdLdg.79572$F_3.60862@newssvr29.news.prodigy.net>`

```
<hcmason@sbcglobal.net> wrote in message
news:1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
> My code in the data division:
> 001900  FD  TEXT-VAR-FILE
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> length really was???

Re-readeth thy doc for the  "RECORD CONTAINS" clause and I think ye shall
find what ye seeketh...

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T22:28:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<riLdg.68101$aA3.37762@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Assuming that you are using VS COBOL II, R3 (with NOCMPR2) *OR* later compiler, 
then there is a solution.  (With OS/VS COBOL there was also a "don't do this - 
but it works" negative subscripting solution).

What you want to do is change

> 001900  FD  TEXT-VAR-FILE
> 002000      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 002500  01  TEXT-VAR-RECORD                PIC X(2071).

to

> 001900  FD  TEXT-VAR-FILE
> 002000      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
> 002100 * I THINK THE REAL LRECL IS 2075, BUT 4 BYTES USED FOR
> 002200 * RECORD LENGTH
> 002300      RECORD Varying in Size from 80 TO 2071
                        depending on Some-item-defined-in-WS.
> 002400  01  TEXT-VAR-REC-80                PIC X(80).
> 002500  01  TEXT-VAR-RECORD                PIC X(2071).

Then in your working storage area define "some-item-defined-in-WS as an integer 
numeric field.  This field will include the actual record length (without the 
"LLZZ" RDW field) after each read.  For more information, check out:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR30/5.2.5.3
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** "P���p��� le Pew" <atchoum@ici.com>
- **Date:** 2006-05-28T21:25:15+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4479f845$0$24465$79c14f64@nan-newsreader-07.noos.net>`
- **References:** `<1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <riLdg.68101$aA3.37762@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> a ï¿½crit dans le message de 
news: riLdg.68101$aA3.37762@fe06.news.easynews.com...
> Assuming that you are using VS COBOL II, R3 (with NOCMPR2) *OR* later 
> compiler, then there is a solution.  (With OS/VS COBOL there was also a 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                        depending on REC-LEN.

>> 002400  1  TEXT-VAR-REC              PIC X(3000).
.....
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

1  REC-LEN     pic 9999.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
.....
        READ TEXT-VAR-FILE  AT END .....

        EVALUATE REC-LEN
            when 80      perform TEXT-VAR-80-ROUTINE
            when 2071  perform TEXT-VAR-2071-ROUTINE
            when other  display "BAD LENGTH RECORD : " REC-LEN
        END-EVALUATE




>
> Then in your working storage area define "some-item-defined-in-WS as an 
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** hcmason@sbcglobal.net
- **Date:** 2006-05-28T12:49:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1148845746.365035.266330@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<riLdg.68101$aA3.37762@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <riLdg.68101$aA3.37762@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```
Dear Bill:
Your suggestion worked.  Awesome!  You are Mr. COBOL..  My reading of
the manual was that you had to include the value in the record itself
before you write it out, and then when the record was read by another
program, that program would "know".  However, your correct
interpretation works...Here is a snippet of the values displayed, the
paragraph names and the ws-size:
002000  FD  TEXT-VAR-FILE
 002100      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
 002200 * I THINK THE REAL LRECL IS 2075, BUT 4 BYTES USED FOR
 002300 * RECORD LENGTH
 002400      RECORD VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON WS-SIZE.
  002600  01  TEXT-VAR-REC-80                PIC X(80).
 002700  01  TEXT-VAR-RECORD                PIC X(2071).
and the variable...
003500      01  WS-SIZE                     PIC 9(04) VALUE ZEROES.
 A0000-MAIN
BEGIN PROGRAM L74HC
 A1000-INIT
Z0000-READ-INPUT
WS-SIZE = 0400
FILE-STATUS IS 00
B0000-PROCESS
B1000-LOOP-THRU-DATA
DATA IS =
DOC5PD00020
Z0000-READ-INPUT
WS-SIZE = 0387
So now I have a way to know home much data to move into the array to be
parsed....
I tried the length of register, but it showed 2071 for everything...I
guess it takes the size of the max record
008110     DISPLAY 'LENGTH OF TEXT-VAR-RECORD = '
 008200         LENGTH OF TEXT-VAR-RECORD.
Thanks Bill.
Chris Mason.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@adelphia.net>
- **Date:** 2006-05-26T21:11:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gYadndj5FqgUTOrZnZ2dnUVZ_tGdnZ2d@adelphia.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1148681398.865250.65430@j55g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
I'd suggest defining the record as a set of characters - PIC X(01) - 
that OCCURS 1 TO 2071 DEPENDING data-name.  Call the 01 level something 
like VAR-REC.

Then, after you have read the record (without an INTO clause), you can 
use the LENGTH OF Special Register to find out how many bytes you read 
for this record, and store that number in "data-name" (the object of the 
OCCURS ... DEPENDING).

After that, you can MOVE the record from the FD to WORKING-STORAGE, and 
it will get padded with spaces if it is shorter than the maximum record 
size.
```

```
Colin,
    Are you sure of this?  I don't think that will work if you have RECORD 
CONTAINS rather than RECORD VARYING IN SIZE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length Records procesing on IBM Mainframes

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

```
You need a DEPENDING ON clause to do this.

It's interesting that as the decades go on, I've come across fewer and
fewer variable length records.    At first they were replaced by
header and detail records, and later by databases.
```
